entry.title: Calling All Applicants for the 2026 Global Delivery Hub Americas High School Summer Orientation!
entry.content:
Each year, SAP’s Global Delivery Hub Americas (formerly Premium Hub CoE NA) hosts high school students to participate in a weeklong Summer Orientation. The 25-hour program provides students with the opportunity to learn about SAP technologies through different activities while receiving educational and career guidance from professionals in the field. SAP experts lead daily, interactive sessions that cover a variety of exciting topics, from coding and data analytics to SAP Cloud, IoT, and Mobile platforms. Over the course of the week, students use their technical skills and newly acquired knowledge to resolve real-world scenarios using SAP technologies. This year we are excited to host the orientation in-person at the Newtown Square campus (outside of Philadelphia). There, students will be able to explore the office, as well as participate in hands-on activities.   

Every year the Summer Orientation features a jam-packed schedule, from expert innovation sessions to career development guidance. The week starts off with a brief introduction of SAP along with a presentation on the various internship opportunities offered to college students. Students also have a career tip session, where they receive professional advice about their educational journey and beyond. Later in the week, students get to meet Balaji Gaddam, SVP Head of Global Delivery Hub Americas. 

Students work on a primary project throughout the week that is always relevant to the latest in technology. The students’ work has evolved over the years, much like how SAP’s products have evolved as well! In previous years of the program, students have built projects using a wide variety of SAP technologies. At the end of the week, students will be able to showcase their project to their fellow peers and Delivery Hub Team members.

High school students interested in attending the 2026 Summer Orientation should fill out the form below and update their most recent transcript. Space is limited for this competitive program and is geared towards rising Juniors and Seniors. Transcripts are required to be considered for the program so they must be uploaded to be considered. No resumes or cover letters are needed. Ideal candidates will have a strong interest in STEM and a desire to pursue further study and/or a career in a STEM-related field.  This is NOT an internship. 

Apply to the STEM Week here with your transcript


Applications will be accepted until Friday, June 12. Students accepted to the program will be notified of the decision by Friday, June 26. The orientation will run in-person at the SAP Newtown Square Campus from July 27 to July 31.  The general hours are 10 am to 3 pm but early dismissal on Friday. Transportation will need to be arranged for the student as we do not provide this. Please note that this is not an internship, it is an unpaid program, and all costs associated with travel will need to be covered by the student. SAP will not cover any costs.

entry.title: Building a Procurement Agent for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Joule Studio – Part 2
entry.content:
TL;DR: This is the second post of a three-part series on building a custom AI agent for Joule, specialized in creating purchase requisitions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through a single prompt. In this second part, we walk through the steps to build Joule Skills and a technique for item parsing using SAP Build Process Automation. Part 1 can be found here.

 

 

Understanding the Joule Skill

A Joule Skill is a modular capability that SAP Joule can invoke to execute a specific task on your behalf. It is a packaged “action” that defines:

  • Which business intent it addresses (e.g., “get allocation runs”, “list projects”);
  • Which data and systems it needs to call (APIs, services);
  • What the expected inputs and outputs are;
  • And how to execute with proper security and governance.

With Skills, in addition to answering questions, Joule can execute tasks across enterprise systems in a predictable and controlled manner.

 

Typical Structure of a Joule Skill

Here are some common elements in the structure of a Joule Skill built in Joule Studio:

  • Intent and description: the business outcome being delivered.
  • Input parameters: what Joule needs to receive (e.g., period, project ID). Skills primarily work with text (string) values for input parameters.
  • Backend action: integration and queries to APIs of SAP or external systems (through Actions).
  • Output schema: the format of the result to be returned to the user or to the next steps.

 

Joule Skill vs. Joule Agent

Compared to classical developments, Joule Skills can be seen as encapsulated functions with deterministic behavior. What does this mean? Given the same input, the expected outcome of executing a Joule Skill will always be the same.

Unlike Joule Agents, which include a reasoning step, Joule Skills follow a defined logical path, with predictable behavior and consistent results when the same input data is provided. They are tools defined through a low-code platform that will be consumed by AI agents.

Joule Agents, by contrast, are flexible and variable. They run on top of an LLM (Large Language Model) and do not follow a fixed logical path. They use AI reasoning to decide what to do, based on the context available to them. They can plan, reason, and dynamically solve problems using the tools at their disposal. They are defined through the system prompt, which will be covered in more detail in the final part of this series.

 

The Importance of Descriptions

Often, when developing a custom object in the ABAP environment, descriptions are an afterthought. How many times have you come across a structure in the data dictionary with technical field names that could not be inferred, and incomplete or barely meaningful descriptions?

When developing Joule Skills and Agents, this changes: descriptions matter. They help the agent, during the reasoning phase, to better understand what they are working with. As a result, detailed descriptions are highly welcome – both for the people who will maintain your artifacts in the future and to help the Agent achieve a higher success rate during execution.
Important: Use precise, clear English descriptions, since user requests – even when expressed in another language – are translated internally into English.

 

Procurement Scenario Considerations

Now that you understand what differentiates a Joule Agent from a Joule Skill, let’s briefly discuss how our building blocks will relate to each other and the call flow between them. The simplified diagram below illustrates the flow:

Simplified message flow through components

 

 

In the proposed scenario, when sending a single prompt, the business user will already provide the items and their respective fields. The Joule Agent will receive this prompt and, based on its definitions, split the information between the header and items, then invoke our Joule Skill to request the creation of the purchase requisition.

As mentioned, Joule Skill works with input parameters in text format. Inside it, however, the received text must be transformed into a list using an item-parsing technique. There are several ways to do this. In our case, we used a Process built with SAP Build Process Automation.

In this automation, the available script tasks were used to parse the items, to call the Products API, and subsequently to map the values back to the ISO unit of measure required by the Purchase Requisition creation API.

The items, now mapped and enriched with their corresponding units of measure, are returned to the Joule Skill, which is then ready to call our Action to create the purchase requisition. After the SAP S/4HANA execution, the PR object is returned and captured by the Joule Skill. Finally, the flow returns the ID of the generated purchase requisition.

The Joule Agent can then capture this information and present it to the business user as a message.

 

Creating a Process for Item Parsing

Roles: developers.

Although they are not strictly objects created by Joule Studio, both Processes and Automations are part of the SAP Build suite and can be reused inside Joule Skills. This allows previously designed flows and automations to also be integrated with Joule’s extended capabilities.

In our example, we need a Process for one specific reason: the purchase requisition creation Action expects items in a tabular format. Therefore, the string carrying the PR item information must be converted into rows. We will use a script task to demonstrate this case.

For didactic purposes, we will also use the Process to incorporate information from the products API and assign the corresponding ISO unit of measure. This information will also be obtained through a script.

 

To create a Process Automation project, access the SAP Build Lobby. Then, click Create. Select Automated Process and then Process. Add a name and a description for your project, review it, and confirm the creation.

Creating a Process Automation project

 

First, we configure the Trigger. This Process will be invoked from a Joule Skill, so it must work via an API call. Add a name and a description.

Adding a process trigger

 

For the case of Purchase Requisition creation, we will need a data type containing all the fields we expect to send to our Action. To create this structure, return to the newly created project and choose Create > Data Type, then provide the name and the description.

Creating a data type

 

In our use case, we created a flat structure similar to the one in the API. The fields included in the structure were:

Name

Type

List

Required

Plant

String

No

Yes

BaseUnit

String

No

No

Material

String

No

Yes

DeliveryDate

Date

No

Yes

RequestedQuantity

String

No

Yes

PurchaseRequisitionItem

String

No

Yes

PurchaseRequisition

String

No

Yes

ZJoule

String

No

No

Note: Remember that the ZJoule field was enabled through key user extensibility. If you are reproducing this example, keep in mind that this field will not exist unless you enable it beforehand. Execution will not be impacted if you have not done so – simply skip this field in all the steps.

 

Back to the Process, we will define the input/output parameters and the custom variables. Process Inputs and Outputs are the parameters received and returned by the Process. Custom variables are variables that exist only during the execution of the automation.

To access this section, click on an empty area and then on the << button.

Editing Process parameters

 

For our scenario, we defined the parameters below:

Parameter type

Name

Description

Type

Required

List

Process Input

Purchase Requisition Items – In

Purchase requisition items – trigger

String

Yes

No

Process Output

Purchase Requisition Items

Parsed purchase requisition items

Purchase Requisition Item

Yes

Yes

Custom Variable

Purchase requisition items

Purchase requisitions items

Purchase Requisition Item

N/A

Yes

Custom Variable

Product filter

Product filter string

String

N/A

No

A few additional comments:

  • “Purchase Requisition Items – In” is the parameter that contains all the items inferred by the Agent, in string format.
  • “Purchase Requisition Items” is the output parameter that will hold the items in list format. Note that we activated the List property for this parameter.
  • “Purchase requisition items” and “Product filter” are, respectively, the variable in which the items will be processed and enriched, and the variable that holds a filter string in the format expected by the OData service.

 

Now, we will start building the process flow. After the trigger, click the + icon to add a step and select the Script Task option. Here, we set the name (“Map purchase requisition items”) and click the Edit Script button to open the editor. Then we insert the JavaScript code below, which performs the item mapping and builds the product filter:

// Purchase requisition items
// Parsing items (string to JSON)
var lines = JSON.parse($.context.startEvent.purchaseRequisitionItemsIn);
var index = 0;

// Function formatDate – receives a date object and returns a string with format “yyyy-mm-dd”
function formatDate(date) {
    var yyyy = date.getFullYear();
    var mm = String(date.getMonth() + 1);
    var dd = String(date.getDate());
    if (mm.length < 2) mm = '0' + mm;
    if (dd.length < 2) dd = '0' + dd;
    return yyyy + '-' + mm + '-' + dd;
}

// From parsed array, map values to the custom variable purchaseRequisitionItems
$.context.custom.purchaseRequisitionItems = lines.map(function(line) {
    index += 10;

    return {
        PurchaseRequisition: "",
        PurchaseRequisitionItem: index.toString(),
        Material: line.Material,
        Plant: line.Plant,
        DeliveryDate: formatDate(new Date(line.DeliveryDate)),
        RequestedQuantity: parseFloat(line.RequestedQuantity).toString(),
        BaseUnit: line.BaseUnit,
        ZJoule: line.ZJoule
    };
});

// Build filter string and set value to variable productFilter
function buildFilterString(lines) {
      var filter = "Product eq '" + lines[0].Material + "'";
      for (var i = 1; i < lines.length; i++) {
          filter += " or Product eq '" + lines[i].Material + "'";
      }
      return filter;
}

$.context.custom.productFilter = buildFilterString(lines);

 Important: The JavaScript code provided must be compatible with ECMAScript 5.1.

 

Creating a script task

Note: If you are not familiar with creating scripts, refer to the page Create and Configure a Script Task

Next, the API to retrieve product/material details will be called. We pick the Action for products created in part 1. When you select it, a new dependency will be added to your project.

Adding an Action to your Process flow

 

 

You will need to create a destination variable (which will be requested when testing and deploying this Process) and the input parameters. We assigned the variable Product filter as the value mapped to the $filter parameter.

 

Then we add a second script that looks up the result in the product list and maps the ISO unit of measure to the Purchase Requisition Items variable.

  var sourceList = $.context.action_get_Product_1.result.value;
  var targetList = $.context.custom.purchaseRequisitionItems;

  // Build a lookup map from Product -> BaseISOUnit
  var sourceMap = {};
  for (var i = 0; i < sourceList.length; i++) {
      var sourceItem = sourceList[i];
      sourceMap[sourceItem.Product] = sourceItem.BaseISOUnit;
  }

  // Map BaseISOUnit to BaseUnit in the target list
  for (var j = 0; j < targetList.length; j++) {
      var targetItem = targetList[j];
      var material = targetItem.Material;
      if (sourceMap.hasOwnProperty(material)) {
          targetItem.BaseUnit = sourceMap[material];
      }
  }

 

Adding the second script task

 

The last step in building the Process is mapping its output. When you click End, the side menu displays the Process Outputs section. Here, the value of the Purchase Requisition Items variable will be transferred to the result. Since both structures are of the same type, all fields will be automatically filled in.

Mapping the Process output

 

The Process is now ready to be saved, released, and deployed to the Easy Purchase Requisition environment. This sequence can be executed by clicking Save and then Release. A pop-up will open, where you can enter the version details. Once released, you can click the generated version and select Deploy, then choose your environment and the destination used internally by the API (in our example, EPRProductV4).

 

Saving, releasing, and deploying a Process

 

Back at the lobby, you must publish your automation. If you do not, it will not be available to your Joule Skill. To publish, select the Process, navigate to the Versions tab, click the “…” button on your Released version, and choose the option Publish to Library.

Publishing a Process

 

Creating a Joule Skill

Roles: functional consultants and developers.

The item-parsing prerequisites have been met by the automation we just generated. Now we can proceed with the creation of a Joule Skill.

First, we go to the project. From the SAP Build Lobby, choose Create > Joule Agent and Skill.

Creating a Joule Agent and Skill project from SAP Build lobby

 

Provide a name and a description for your project.

Project name and description

If you need additional information on how to proceed with project creation, refer to the official documentation.

 

When the project opens, select Create > Joule Skill.

Creating a Joule Skill

 

In the pop-up, give your new capability a meaningful name and a detailed description of the purpose it covers. Remember: descriptions help the agent identify the most relevant case for a request from your business user. You should include information about the goal and the parameters expected for its execution.

Joule Skill - initial parameters

 

For our use case, we set the name to “Create a Purchase Requisition” and the description to “A skill to create any type of purchase requisition, based on the PR type and items described through a single prompt, including custom fields (e.g. ZJoule)”.

 

Structuring a Joule Skill is very similar to structuring a Process or an Automation in SAP Build. You will have access to a low-code tool to structure the process flow. To learn everything you can do in a Joule Skill, refer to the documentation Create a Joule Skill.

 

First, we need to define the behavior of the Joule Skill. By clicking anywhere on the background and then on the << button, the configurations will be displayed. In the General tab, you will find the name and description proposed earlier. If necessary, you can also correct or enhance them. There are also two switches:

  • Allow Joule to generate a response: when enabled, this option allows Joule to extract information from the skill and turn it into a response. If you decide not to enable it, you will need to configure the “Send Message” steps to deliver the response. For more information on managing responses, refer to “Send a Response”.
  • Allow skill to be started directly by a user: when enabled, this option allows the capability to be invoked directly by the user, i.e., without an agent or another capability acting as an intermediary. When this option is disabled, the Joule Skill must necessarily have at least one output parameter.

General tab - Joule Skill

 

For this use case, we used:

  • Allow Joule to generate a response: enabled. This will allow Joule to capture the capability’s return and compose its own response.
  • Allow skill to be started directly by a user: disabled. Since there is a step that captures and stringifies the items with a specific structure, it is not advisable to allow the user to invoke the skill directly. We will delegate to the Joule Agent the task of formatting the parameters and invoking the capability.

 

On the Parameters tab, just as we did for the Process, we define the input and output parameters and variables.

Setting input and output parameters

 

In our procurement example, the parameters were configured according to the table below:

Parameter type

Name

Description

Type

Required

List

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Items

Purchase requisition items as a stringified array

String

Yes

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Type

Purchase requisition type

String

Yes

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Description

Purchase requisition description

String

No

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Header Note

Purchase requisition header note

String

No

No

Skill Output

Purchase Requisition ID

Generated purchase requisition ID

String

No

No

Skill Variable

Purchase Requisition ID

Purchase requisition ID

String

N/A

Yes

 

After completing the parameter configuration, we will create the flow to be executed. To do so, click the + symbol and insert the steps, mapping the inputs and outputs of each activity type.

 Adding a Process task to Joule Skill

 

In our example, we started by inserting the Process Easy Purchase Requisition – Item Parsing in order to transform and enrich the items described by the input parameter. We requested that execution be awaited until completion so that the process could return its output, containing a list of items with their respective ISO units of measure mapped.

The table below summarizes the configuration applied to this step:

Parameter type

Name

Value

General

Step Name

Map Items

General

Wait for Output Result

true

Input

Purchase Requisition Items – In

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition items

 

Adding an Action call

 

Mapping Action parameters

 

Next, we inserted the Action (API) call to create a purchase requisition. Similar to what was done in the Process for the products Action, here we mapped the inputs and outputs. We also defined an environment variable that holds the destination name to be used by the API call.

The following table describes this mapping:

Parameter type

Name

Value

General

Step Name

Create a purchase requisition

General

Destination Value

DESTINATION_PR

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem (Bind List)

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > BaseUnitISOCode

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > BaseUnit

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > DeliveryDate

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > DeliveryDate

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > Material

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > Material

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > Plant

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > Plant

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > PurchaseRequisition

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > PurchaseRequisition

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > PurchaseRequisitionItem

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > PurchaseRequisitionItem

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > RequestedQuantity

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > RequestedQuantity

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > ZJoule

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > ZZ1_ZJoule_PRI

Input

PurchaseRequisition

Custom variables > Purchase requisition ID

Input

PurchaseRequisitionType

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition type

Input

PurchaseReqnDescription

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition description

Input

PurReqnHeaderNote

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition header note

 

Setting variables and Skill output

 

The last step assigns the resulting purchase requisition ID value, generated through the API call, to a local variable using the Set Variables activity.

Section

Name

Value

General

Step Name

Set purchase requisition ID

General > Set Custom Variable

Purchase requisition ID

Create a purchase requisition > result > PurchaseRequisition

 

Then we set the value of this same variable as our Skill Output.

Mapping the Skill output

 

Click Save to store your Joule Skill. You don’t need to release or deploy it right now, since we are still creating the Joule Agent in part 3.

 

Key Takeaways

If you have made it this far and followed the example, you now know how to:

  • Identify the structure of a Joule Skill.
  • Distinguish a Joule Skill from a Joule Agent (deterministic vs. reasoning).
  • Create a Process or an Automation and use these objects to support item parsing.
  • Create a project for Joule Skills and Agents and start building a Joule Skill.
  • Consume Actions and build a logical flow for your custom capability, mapping inputs and outputs.

Always remember the importance of properly describing the capability and its parameters to facilitate the agent’s inference process.

 

Next Steps

In the next and final post in this series, we will cover the creation of the Joule Agent. There, we will use the artifacts from this guide to enable the Joule Agent to perform the desired operation. We will also walk through the process of making your agent available, allowing it to coexist with the standard Joule capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Thanks for reading, and see you there!

 

Thanks,
Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG Team

entry.title: AI Product Management: Autonomy Is Earned, Not Scheduled
entry.content:
AI Product Management series

Here is a sentence that appears, in some form, in most agentic rollout plans: "After thirty days of supervised operation, we will move the agent to full autonomy." Or "after five hundred clean actions." Or "following the first quarterly review."

Every one of those is a schedule. None of them is a safety criterion. A schedule measures how much time has passed or how many actions have accumulated. It does not measure whether the agent has demonstrated, in the failure modes that actually matter for this decision type, that it can be trusted with more authority. Confusing the two is one of the most common and most expensive governance mistakes in agentic AI, and it is worth taking apart.

The autonomy ladder

In deterministic SaaS you were trained to think in terms of feature completeness. A workflow was either automated or it was not. For agents, full automation is not a starting point. It is an earned state, and the climb to it has rungs.

Rung 1 is augmentation. The agent surfaces information and prepares options; the human decides. The PM's job here is the quality of the recommendation and how uncertainty is communicated, so you do not create blind trust disguised as assistance.

Rung 2 is limited automation with approval gates. The agent executes bounded actions after human confirmation. The PM's job is the approval moment: the decision package the human sees, how uncertainty and alternatives appear, and what happens if nobody responds within the defined window.

Rung 3 is semi-autonomous operation with guardrails. The agent acts within a defined scope and escalates outside it. The PM's job is the escalation triggers, the audit surface, the recovery workflow, and, critically, the evidence required before the system earns this level of authority.

The rungs are not a timeline. They are a sequence gated by demonstrated competence. Movement up requires demonstrated performance at the rung below, documented evidence of safe operation, and explicit criteria for what "safe enough" means for this specific decision type.

What scheduling actually measures

Most teams in practice schedule the climb. They set a date or a threshold count, and when it arrives, the agent moves up. This feels rigorous because it is quantified. A number is involved. A milestone is hit.

But look at what the number measures. Thirty days measures the passage of time. Five hundred clean actions measures volume of activity. Neither measures whether the agent has been tested against the cases that determine whether it is safe at the next rung. An agent can run five hundred clean refunds and never once encounter the fraud-pattern case that is the entire reason a human boundary exists. Scheduling promotes it anyway, on day thirty-one, into authority over exactly the cases it has never been shown to handle.

The clean record is not evidence of competence on the hard cases. It is usually evidence that the hard cases have not come up yet. Time in production is not the same as coverage of the failure surface. A schedule conflates them, and that conflation is where the incident comes from.

This is governance theater: the appearance of a careful, criteria-based promotion process, built on a criterion that does not measure the thing it is supposed to gate.

What earned promotion requires instead

Earned autonomy replaces the calendar with evidence. To move an agent up a rung, you require three things, defined before launch, not negotiated after the first incident.

First, demonstrated performance at the current rung, measured against the failure modes that matter for this decision type, not against aggregate volume. The question is not "how many actions has it taken?" but "has it been tested, repeatedly, on the specific cases where being wrong is expensive, and did it hold?" This is where your eval set earns its keep: it is the curated collection of hard cases, including the never-ship failures, that the agent must clear before promotion is even on the table.

Second, documented evidence of safe operation, meaning the audit surface actually produced the instruments that let you see how the agent behaved, not just that it completed tasks. The gap between task completion and genuine success, the unintended-action rate, the override pattern on escalations: these are what tell you whether the clean record reflects competence or absence of hard cases.

Third, an explicit, written definition of "safe enough" for the next rung. Safe enough is not universal. The threshold for promoting a support-ticket categorizer is not the threshold for promoting an agent with payment authority. Write the threshold down before launch, because a threshold defined after the first incident is defined under pressure, by people who want the problem to go away.

The piece nobody writes down: demotion triggers

There is a direction on the ladder that rollout plans almost never address. They specify the climb. They never specify the fall.

Before launch, define the demotion triggers: the thresholds on unintended actions, override frequency, or incident severity that force the agent back down a rung. What rate of wrong autonomous actions pulls it from rung 3 to rung 2? What override pattern says the humans no longer trust it and the boundary needs to tighten? What single incident is severe enough to demote on its own?

If demotion criteria are not defined before launch, they will be defined under pressure after the first incident, which is the worst possible time to be designing a control. In the moment, there is an outage, a frustrated executive, and a team improvising a response to a situation nobody planned for. The demotion trigger written calmly in advance is a control. The one improvised during an incident is damage control.

An agent that can only climb is not on a ladder. It is on an escalator with no stop button.

Three decisions, one discipline

Earned autonomy is not a single gate. It is the same discipline applied at three points in the lifecycle.

Pre-build, you decide whether the problem is suitable for an agent at all. Pre-launch, you decide whether the agent has earned its starting rung, the level of authority it ships with on day one, which for a consequential decision type is rarely the top of the ladder. Post-launch, you decide, repeatedly, whether it has earned the next one, on evidence, against criteria you wrote before you had any reason to want a particular answer.

Three decisions, one discipline: authority follows demonstrated competence, in the failure modes that matter, measured against criteria set in advance. Not a date. Not a count of clean runs. Evidence that the agent can be trusted with the specific cases the next rung exposes it to.

The calendar is comfortable because it is automatic. It promotes the agent without anyone having to make a judgment call. That is exactly the problem. The judgment call is the job. An agent's authority over consequential decisions is precisely the thing that should never be on autopilot, and a schedule is autopilot wearing the costume of a process.

entry.title: AI Product Management: The Two Documents That Replace the PRD
entry.content:
AI Product Management series

For twenty years the artifact was the PRD. You wrote it, handed it to engineering, watched it get decomposed into an epic, and the team built it. That model worked because the hard part was building. With agentic AI, the hard part is no longer building. A coding agent will produce a working prototype of almost anything you can describe in an afternoon. The hard part is deciding what the agent should do, where its authority stops, and how the people around it will know when it is wrong.

So the artifact splits in two. And the most common way good agent projects go wrong is that the second document gets written and the first never does.

This post is about both documents and the discipline of writing them in the right order.

The decision and the build are different documents

Call them the Human Brief and the Executable Brief.

The Human Brief is the document a room argues with. It is the PRD's successor, written in prose: the business case, the go/no-go, the cost model, where the boundary sits and why. Its audience is the executive sponsor, finance, engineering, design, and the domain expert. Its purpose is to decide whether to build at all, before anyone writes code.

The Executable Brief is the document a system acts on. It is the epic's successor, written as numbered, testable requirements: the behavior, the supervisory experience, and the governance, precise enough to generate a working prototype this week and seed a backlog next. Its audience is the prototype, the coding agent, and the team.

You derive the second from the first. The decision comes before the specification, always, and skipping the Human Brief is how a team ends up six months into building something nobody ever decided was worth building.

What both briefs share

Here is the part that is easy to get wrong. The temptation is to put the agent in one document and the supervision in the other. That is exactly how the supervision ends up unbuilt.

Both briefs carry both channels: Channel 1, the agent and what it does, and Channel 2, the system that supervises it. The split between the two briefs is by audience and purpose, decide versus build, not by channel.

In the Human Brief, the supervisory layer appears as a decision the room owns: where the boundary sits, what it costs to fund the oversight honestly, who is accountable for a wrong outcome. In the Executable Brief, the same supervisory layer appears as buildable requirements: the autonomy boundary, the approval experience, the audit surface, the eval set. The supervision gets built because it was written into the spec as a requirement, not because someone remembered it at the end.

The Human Brief: the document you argue with

A Human Brief contains, in prose a room can debate:

The problem and the opportunity. What is costing money or going wrong today, in one honest paragraph, including the size.

What you are building, and what you are deliberately not. The intended behavior in plain terms, and the adjacent thing it must not become, which is usually the failure mode of the obvious version. "Resolve refunds automatically" must not quietly become "approve refunds the company should have questioned."

The cases that decide whether it is worth building. The hard cases the agent must handle well, not the easy ones that were never the problem. The in-window defective item was always going to be refunded; the agent earns nothing there. It earns its place on the request just outside the window where the customer is right on the merits, the high-value account near churn, the amount ten times the others on an account that smells like fraud.

The business case and the go/no-go. The suitability tests, the cost model with real numbers, and an explicit decision gate. This is the section a finance partner will press on, and the place where the trap lives: the model's token cost per decision is a few cents, far below a human's time, and that is not the real cost. The real cost is the architecture multiplier, orchestration, integrations, the audit pipeline, eval maintenance, plus the human reviewing every escalated case. The break-even is not "agent cheaper than human per task." It is "fully loaded cost, including supervising the escalated minority, below the cost of humans reviewing all of it, with the wrongful-action risk priced in." If the only way the math works is by under-funding the human review, the answer is no.

Where the boundary sits, and why. The autonomy limit and the escalation triggers, stated as a deliberate choice with its risk named. This is the Channel 2 decision the room owns. You are choosing, explicitly, which kind of wrong is tolerable and which is never tolerable even once. That asymmetry is what the room should argue about.

Accountability and how you will know. Who owns a wrong outcome, named before launch, not in the incident review by pointing at whoever is nearest. And the instruments that will signal drift before the loss shows up.

What success looks like, and what it does not. The real definition of done, written to exclude the seductive wrong metric, which for most agents is "share resolved without a human." An agent that resolves everything has almost certainly approved things it should have questioned.

The open questions the room still owes. The business decisions not yet made, each flagged in the open. A question named here is one the room knows it must answer. A question left out is one that gets answered by default, in code, by whoever ships first.

The Executable Brief: the document a system acts on

The Executable Brief takes three disciplines from spec-driven development tooling (GitHub's Spec Kit is the clearest current instance) and adds the part those tools do not give you.

Requirements are numbered and written as testable statements, not prose, so each can be checked off rather than interpreted. Every place the spec is silent gets an explicit clarification marker rather than a quiet assumption, because that marker is the difference between a gap you decided to leave and a gap the coding agent fills for you with whatever the simplest path suggests. And the behavior is graded against acceptance scenarios in given/when/then form a machine can run.

The structure is borrowed. The supervisory content, the Channel 2 requirements, is the part this brief exists to add.

It contains the system type (suggestion engine, copilot, or autonomous actor, because each carries a different accountability model); an outcome spec stated as a target over a distribution of cases, not a pass/fail story; the behavior as numbered functional requirements (FR-1, FR-2 ...); acceptance scenarios including the hard ones; the supervisory experience, what the human sees and does at the approval moment; the governance as numbered requirements (GR-1, GR-2 ...) covering the autonomy boundary, audit surface, and recovery workflow; measured success criteria stated as numbers over the distribution; the eval set with the endorsed outcome for each case; and the gate the spec must pass before it proceeds.

One functional requirement is worth seeing, because it is the whole point of the exercise. For a refund agent: When the customer's stated reason conflicts with the order record, the resolution is unresolved and flagged for clarification: trust the record, trust the customer, or escalate. The coding agent must not pick for us. That conflict is a real decision with real exposure. Leaving it unmarked is how it gets decided silently, in code, by whoever takes the simplest path.

And one governance requirement that names the non-negotiable: The boundary must be enforced in the execution path before the action fires, not logged after. A boundary that lives in the prompt is a suggestion. A boundary in the payment path is a control.

Why not just write a user story?

A working PM will object that this is a lot of apparatus for what used to be a user story and an acceptance criterion. The comparison is the whole argument.

The old way writes the refund behavior as a story. As a customer, when I request a refund for a defective item, the agent issues the refund, so that I am made whole without waiting. It is clean. It would pass grooming in any backlog in the world. And it is the wrong primitive, because it describes the case that was never hard. What the story cannot say is what the agent should do with the request just outside the window, or the churning customer worth more than the refund, or the amount that smells like fraud. Those are the cases the agent is actually judged on, and the story has no field for any of them.

The reason is structural. The user story works because ordinary software is deterministic: one correct output, and a test that runs once and passes is a real answer. None of that holds for an agent's judgment. The output is a distribution. "Correct" is a judgment, not a binary. A test that passes once tells you little, because the next run may differ.

So the work-unit for the agent's behavior shifts from the user story to an outcome-centric spec: the outcome you want, the bounds the agent must stay inside, and the eval set that grades whether it got there, including what acceptable failure looks like. A deterministic spec says do X then Y. An outcome-centric spec says achieve this outcome, within these bounds, and here is how we will know.

The user story is not dead. The deterministic shell around the agent, the orchestration, the integrations, the screens the user clicks, is still ordinary software and still gets ordinary stories. What resists the story is the probabilistic core, the agent's judgment itself. The signal to watch for: when you find yourself writing an acceptance criterion for an agent's judgment and it reads as pass/fail, that is the deterministic tool on the probabilistic core, and the case you are not writing down is usually the one that reaches the person the agent affects.

The operating model in one breath

Decide in the Human Brief. Specify in the Executable Brief. Build the agent inside the boundary the brief set. Watch it for as long as it runs. Everything downstream, the runtime design, the evals, the production observation, is the brief's requirements made real, and after launch the supervisory track keeps running while the rest winds down.

Two documents, in order. Write the second one without the first, and you have built something well that nobody decided to build.

entry.title: AI Product Management: What Happens to Agents Nobody Plans to Stop
entry.content:
Post 9 — AI Product Management series

In 2021, a research team at the University of Michigan published an audit of the most widely deployed sepsis prediction algorithm in American hospitals. Epic Systems had marketed the Epic Sepsis Model with a claimed area under the curve of 0.76 to 0.83. The Michigan team measured an actual AUC of 0.63. Sensitivity was 33 percent. Positive predictive value, 12 percent.

The algorithm had been live across hundreds of hospitals for six to eight years. No regulator had audited it. Epic had not. The hospitals had not. The audit that finally measured it was an academic exercise, funded by research curiosity, not triggered by any market or compliance mechanism.

The number that stays with you is not 0.63. It is six to eight years.

The mechanism

Silent degradation is not a clinical problem. It is the default trajectory of every deployed agentic system in every domain, because the mechanism is not biological or regulatory. It is structural.

On the system side, drift is not one thing. The foundation model version changes without announcement from the provider. The training cutoff moves. The upstream API evolves. The retrieval corpus shifts. The system prompt accretes small improvements, each tested individually, never tested in combination. The guardrail policy tightens and a workflow that used to complete now pauses at a new threshold. Each substrate has its own drift rate. The agent is a composition of all of them, and the composition amplifies rather than dampens variation.

On the human side, trust in the system rises with time in deployment, even when the system is not getting better. Users watch it appear to work, and the appearance accumulates into assumed reliability. Meanwhile, vigilance falls. Alert fatigue at the interface layer, automation complacency at the workflow layer, automation bias at the decision point. All three operating simultaneously.

Behind both of those vectors, two that are rarely named. The observation system itself degrades as the team rotates, the monitoring dashboard depreciates, the PM who knew which metrics mattered leaves, and the organizational memory of why the observation existed erodes. And the human workaround silently compensates: workers learn the agent's quirks, prompt around the failures, double-check the task types that have burned them before. Their metric, did I finish the task, stays green. The agent's metric, did the AI do its job, was never being measured.

Post 6 described the scissors dynamic at launch: accuracy trends down, trust trends up, and errors go undetected after the lines cross. The Epic Sepsis Model is that chart run out six years.

What agentic systems add

Classical software drifts too. Schemas evolve, dependencies break, and feature flags accumulate until nobody remembers which combinations are tested. The difference is not that vertical systems are stable. The difference is that they fail loudly. An error code, a stack trace, a broken page. Something announces itself.

Agentic systems fail differently. They produce fluent wrong answers. There is no error state that announces degradation. The system continues operating, generating confident output, logging clean metrics, while actual task performance declines below the baseline that justified deployment.

And the failure surface is combinatorial in a way vertical systems are not. A traditional tool has N code paths; you test them. An agent navigates a decision tree where each node is a probabilistic call to a model the vendor may have silently updated, calling tools whose APIs may have silently changed, against a retrieval corpus someone may have silently modified. Compound eight drift rates rather than sum them: that is the number that determines whether the agent is still doing what it was evaluated to do.

The failure mode is fluent-and-wrong, not loud-and-broken. That is why the instrument question is structural, not optional.

The observation instruments as infrastructure

Post 6 introduced the six observation instruments: task success rate, unintended action rate, override frequency, confidence calibration, rollback time, and incident recovery time. The framing there was that these must be designed before launch. If a metric cannot be defined before launch, the design artifact it measures is missing.

Silent degradation adds a second requirement on top: these instruments are not static dashboards. They are infrastructure, and they have a maintenance cost that most teams do not budget for.

Every observation instrument is calibrated against a behavioral baseline established at launch. When the foundation model is updated by the provider, the baseline shifts. The instrument may still return a green reading against the old baseline while the agent's actual performance has changed relative to what users need. The instrument has not failed. It is measuring the wrong thing because the reference point has moved.

The practical consequence: each observation instrument must be versioned alongside the model it measures. When the model changes, the baseline must be re-established, the thresholds must be re-validated, and the instrument must be re-calibrated before the metrics are trusted again. A model update from the provider is not a background event. It is a measurement event that requires a deliberate response.

The teams that will avoid the 2028 equivalent of the Epic Sepsis audit are the ones who treat this re-calibration as a scheduled maintenance event, on the clock of the next model generation, not as a reaction to an incident.

Retirement as lifecycle, not emergency

Retirement is the phase almost no team plans for and everyone eventually needs.

When it is unplanned, it looks like this: the agent produces a serious error. Someone escalates. The response is to shut it down as quickly as possible while managing damage. The team is improvising under pressure with no pre-defined process, no preserved audit trail, and no institutional record of what conditions led to the shutdown.

Crisis-driven retirement produces data loss, user confusion, audit gaps, and no learning. The team that improvised a retirement once will improvise the next one the same way.

Designed retirement has four steps, executed in sequence with named owners assigned before launch.

Step 1: Preserve the audit trail. Before any systems are decommissioned, archive the complete action trace under the retention policy. This step cannot be deferred to after the team figures out what to do. Once infrastructure is decommissioned, the audit record may be permanently unrecoverable. The legal exposure from a missing audit trail in a regulated context often exceeds the exposure from the incident that triggered the retirement.

Step 2: Block new invocations. From the moment the retirement decision is made, the agent accepts no new tasks. In-flight work is either completed under heightened human review or handed to a human workflow. Do not allow new work to enter an agent that is being retired.

Step 3: Notify affected users. Plain-language explanation of why the agent is stopping, when it will stop, and what the replacement workflow is. Workers who have adapted their process around the agent need enough lead time to readapt. The notice that comes the day before shutdown is not this.

Step 4: Document the decision. The reason for retirement, who authorized it, which policy version was in effect, and what specific conditions would need to change before a replacement is considered. This document is the institutional memory that prevents the same design from being reproposed six months later without the context of why the previous version was retired.

None of these steps are complicated. All of them are skipped when retirement is improvised under pressure. Write them into the go/no-go memo as a named owner and a deadline before the agent ships.

The retirement criteria problem

There is a test for whether an agentic deployment has been designed responsibly, and it is simpler than any governance framework.

Ask: what would have to happen for this agent to stop running?

If the answer requires a committee meeting, an executive decision, or a significant incident, the agent has no defined retirement criteria. It will run until something forces it to stop. That something is almost always worse than a designed exit would have been.

The retirement criteria belong in the go/no-go memo alongside the launch criteria. They are the symmetric answer to the launch question: the same rigor applied to the question of when the agent should not be running that was applied to the question of whether it should launch. A pass rate that falls below a defined threshold across consecutive evaluation runs. A suitability condition from the go/no-go assessment that no longer holds. A scope drift outside the authorized mandate that cannot be corrected within the existing governance framework.

These criteria exist before the first incident. They are not derived from the incident that triggers them.

The agent with no retirement criteria is not a risk that might materialize. It is the default outcome of every agentic deployment where this question was deferred to later. Later does not come with better information. It comes with more users, more accumulated audit gaps, and a harder exit.

What the audit trail is actually for

Most audit trails in enterprise agentic systems are built for engineers diagnosing failures. They serve that purpose adequately. They do not serve the two purposes that matter most in a retirement or regulatory review.

The first is the authority delegation chain. For every consequential action the agent took, three questions must be answerable from the audit trail without calling anyone: whose authority was the agent acting under, which version of the governance policy was in effect at the time of the action, and who carries the outcome if the action was wrong. These three fields, if blank, are not a governance gap. They are evidence that the system was operating without governance.

The second is the affected-person record. The customer whose order was affected, the vendor whose payment the agent held, the employee whose request the agent denied, is asking different questions than the engineer: what did the agent decide, on what basis, and what can I do about it. Most enterprise audit trails cannot produce this view. The design work to produce it is modest. The decision to build it before a regulator requests it is a posture question.

Practice Fusion paid $145 million in 2019 to settle federal allegations that it had embedded opioid prescribing suggestions in clinical workflows, funded by a manufacturer, without disclosure. The prompt worked exactly as designed. The governance failure was that none of the three authority delegation fields were populated: no named authorizer, no policy version, no named outcome carrier. Any enterprise deployment where the agent surfaces recommendations without a traceable chain from the recommendation to a named authorizer under a versioned policy carries the same structural gap, regardless of industry.

Planning for the end

There is a list that will exist in 2028 of enterprise agentic systems that were doing something in production that no one on the team knew about, for months or years, until an audit, a regulator, or a customer surfaced it.

The Epic Sepsis Model is already on that list. It took six to eight years, and the mechanism that exposed it was not a market signal or a compliance trigger. It was academic curiosity.

Your deployment does not get a Michigan audit team. It gets the observation infrastructure you built, the behavioral baselines you maintained, the retirement criteria you wrote before launch, and the audit trail that can answer the three authority questions from the record alone.

None of this requires new technology. All of it requires decisions made before the agent ships, not after.

For the full framework, including the drift and retirement policy template and the authority delegation checklist: Agentic AI for Product Managers

entry.title: AI Product Management: The Human Side of Agentic AI Nobody Designs
entry.content:
Post 8 — AI Product Management series

In December 1978, United Airlines Flight 173 ran out of fuel while circling Portland, Oregon. The aircraft was mechanically sound. The crew knew the fuel state was critical. The captain, fixated on a landing gear indicator that might have been faulty, could not integrate what the gauges were telling him. The accident report was not about mechanical failure. It was about a new kind of failure: a technically proficient crew that had never been trained to supervise an increasingly automated cockpit as a system.

Most enterprise agentic deployments are building the same problem and calling it a rollout plan.

The second channel nobody designs

Every agentic product requires two design channels. Most teams design one.

The first channel is the agent: what it does, how its autonomy boundary is defined, what it logs, what it surfaces for review. Most teams design this channel well because it maps onto existing product practice. Specifications, sprint planning, acceptance criteria.

The second channel is the human experience of supervising the agent. How does a person know what the agent is doing? How do they develop the skill to catch its errors? How does the supervisory role fit into their existing workflow? How do they stay actively engaged rather than drifting toward passive monitoring?

Most vendors leave Channel 2 to the customer: a training module, a communication plan, a utilization target handed off at go-live. That is not supervision design. It is adoption tracking. The distinction matters because the problem that will surface at month six is not that people refused to use the agent. It is that they stopped watching it.

What happens to the job

Deploying an agent that acts does not just change the software. It changes the job of every person who interacts with it.

The procurement manager who used to process orders is now supervising the system that processes them. The compliance analyst who used to flag exceptions is now auditing whether the agent flagged the right ones. These are not the same jobs. They are new jobs wearing the old jobs' names.

The cognitive shift required is real and not small. The person who processed orders was making decisions. The person who supervises the agent that processes orders must maintain situational awareness across a system they do not control, at a decision rate that far exceeds what any human could have produced manually, while staying engaged enough to catch the failures the system does not self-report.

This is harder than the original job. It requires a different kind of attention. And it degrades in a specific, predictable way: when the system appears to be working correctly, the supervisor's attention drifts. The agent's accuracy may be declining. The trust is rising. Post 6 described what happens when those two lines cross.

What aviation learned

The Flight 173 accident investigation produced what became Crew Resource Management. CRM does not teach pilots to fly. It teaches them to supervise: situational awareness across the whole cockpit system, communication across authority gradients, structured handoffs at every consequential transition.

Two findings from CRM research apply directly to agentic AI product design.

First, a single training event does not produce durable supervisory behavior. Early CRM programs found that attitude shifts measured immediately after training faded within months. The supervisory culture that actually holds is maintained through recurrent practice, embedded in annual training and live flight audits conducted under normal operating conditions, not crisis conditions. You cannot certify a supervisory culture. You maintain it.

Second, the supervision tools that survived in aviation are the ones that slotted into workflow moments that already existed. Repeating back a proposed action before execution. Structured briefings before high-workload phases. The two-challenge rule that empowers any crew member to stop a decision they are concerned about. These are not new processes layered on top of flying. They are communication patterns inserted at transitions that were already there.

The design requirement for enterprise agentic products is the same: find where in the existing workflow the supervision moment naturally belongs and build the interface there. An approval moment and a confidence signal that appear where the worker is already making decisions will be used. A monitoring dashboard that competes for attention with the actual work will not be.

The three failure modes

The first failure mode is not resistance. It is selective trust.

Workers do not reject AI wholesale. They trust it for low-stakes decisions and disengage from it for high-stakes ones. This is precisely backwards from where algorithmic assistance is most valuable. Research on algorithm aversion shows that trust collapses after a single observed failure far faster than it accumulates through many correct outputs. The design response is not a better communication plan. It is an interface that makes reliability visible, uncertainty legible, and errors catchable before they compound.

The second failure mode is shadow workflows. When the supervisory role is poorly designed, teams maintain parallel manual processes alongside the agent. This is not sabotage. It is rational risk management by people who have not been given a supervision interface they trust. The agent is deployed, the utilization metrics look acceptable, and the workforce is running a second process in parallel. The cost is invisible to dashboards and substantial in practice.

The third failure mode is slower and harder to reverse. When an agent takes over tasks humans used to perform, the capacity to recognize when the agent performs them incorrectly degrades over time. Aviation research found that basic procedural skills remained largely intact after extended autopilot use. What deteriorated were the cognitive and strategic skills: situational awareness, the ability to recognize automation failures before they cascaded. The supervisor does not forget how to do the job. They forget how to think about the job. And unlike the first two failure modes, this one is invisible to the person experiencing it.

The accountability gap

A clinical decision support system analyzes a pathology result and returns: no malignancy detected. The physician sees the recommendation, confirms it, and moves on. Eighteen months later the patient is diagnosed with stage IV cancer.

Who is responsible?

The vendor points to the physician who approved the output. The physician notes that the system was cleared and integrated by the hospital. The hospital references the vendor's accuracy claims. The regulator notes the system performed within labeled parameters. Nobody is clearly culpable. The patient cannot get a legible explanation of what the model concluded or why.

This is the structure of every accountability failure in supervised AI systems. Andreas Matthias named three distinct gaps in 2004: the culpability gap, the public accountability gap, and the active responsibility gap. They are not fixed by naming someone in a governance document. They are fixed by designing a system where one named person has a legible view of what the agent decided, on what grounds, with what confidence, and carries that record into the outcome.

Research confirms that when AI is involved in consequential decisions, human supervisors feel less personally responsible for errors, even when they approved the action. The more automated the pathway, the more responsibility feels shared, and the less any individual feels obligated to catch errors. That diffusion does not respond to policy. It responds to design: a supervision interface that makes the authorization explicit, the basis legible, and the accountability named.

The affected-person audit view

Most audit trails are designed for engineers diagnosing a failure. They contain system logs, API call records, error codes, and timing data. They are not readable by anyone without an engineering background, and they do not answer the question the affected person is actually asking.

The affected person, the customer whose order was delayed, the vendor whose payment was held, the employee whose application was rejected, is asking a different question: why did this happen, was it correct, and what can I do about it?

Most enterprise agentic systems cannot answer this question. There is no designed path from the agent's decision to a plain-language explanation accessible to the person the decision affected.

Build the affected-person audit view before a regulator requires it. It uses the same underlying action trace as the engineering audit but presents it differently: a plain-language description of what the agent decided, the basis for that decision, the authority under which it acted, and the options available to the affected person if they believe the decision was wrong. In an increasing number of regulated environments, including GDPR and various state-level privacy laws, the right to a meaningful explanation of automated decisions is moving from a design preference to a compliance obligation.

The engineering audit and the affected-person audit are the same underlying data. The design work is the presentation layer. Most teams build only the first one and call their audit requirement satisfied.

The practical test

There is a simple test for whether a team has designed both channels.

Ask for the override frequency metric: what percentage of agent actions are reviewed and modified by human supervisors, in which workflows, over what period. If the team cannot produce this number, the approval moment was not designed to be logged. The supervisory system cannot be improved without data on how it is being used.

Ask for the supervision training design: not the completion rate, but whether the training includes practice managing the process when the agent is wrong or unavailable. If it does not exist, Channel 2 was handed to the customer at go-live and was never a product decision.

Ask whether shadow workflows exist. They will not appear in utilization dashboards. They show up in the gap between what the dashboard reports and what the work actually looks like on the floor.

One finding from clinical supervisory research is worth naming directly: structured supervision in surgical ICUs improved outcomes but increased the average duration of rapid response team events by 33 percent. Good supervision was not faster. It was more thorough. A temporary slowdown after agentic rollout is not a failure signal. It is evidence that supervision is actually happening. The metric to watch is not speed. It is whether errors are being caught before they compound.

Two channels, one product

The anesthesiologist who trusts the ventilator enough to focus on the blood pressure trend is not failing to supervise. She is doing the harder work that the trust made possible. The design question is how the system holds that trust stable, surfaces the moment it is eroding, and keeps the supervisory skill available for when it is needed.

You built the agent. Now design the person who is supposed to be watching it. That is half the product, and most teams never open the specification for it.

For the full framework, including the go/no-go memo template, runtime artifact checklist, and drift and retirement policy template: Agentic AI for Product Managers

entry.title: The RAG Chunking Strategies that can actually survive Production
entry.content:
The Chunking Strategies That Actually Survive Production

If your RAG quality has plateaued, the bottleneck is almost never your vector DB or your reranker. It is almost always your chunker. The naive splitters in every quickstart tutorial destroy the one thing retrieval depends on: context.

This is a tour of the chunking strategies you will actually meet in production. For each one I cover how it works, why you would reach for it, why you would not, and where it fits. I am avoiding hard benchmark numbers on purpose - the field moves fast and figures age badly. What does not age is the mechanism each strategy relies on, and the corpus shape it was built for.

Why Chunking Is The Hidden Lever

A retriever can only return what your indexer produced. If your chunks are mid-sentence fragments stripped of context, no embedder, reranker, or LLM downstream will recover what you destroyed at ingest. Most teams over-invest in retrieval and under-invest in chunking. The fix is upstream.

Two failure modes show up over and over:

  1. The fragment problem. A chunk reading "set the timeout to 30 seconds in the advanced settings tab" is meaningless without knowing what product, what version, and which settings tab.
  2. The boundary problem. A chunk that ends mid-table, mid-code-block, or mid-sentence has lost the structural cue the author wrote precisely to communicate meaning.

Every strategy below is a different answer to those two problems.

Strategy 1: Fixed-Size Chunking

Workflow. Cut the document every N characters or N tokens, optionally with M characters of overlap. No grammar, no structure, no embeddings. Pure counting.

Example. A document about Kubernetes controllers gets sliced every 512 characters. One chunk ends with "the controller watches for chan" and the next begins with "ges and reconciles state". Both chunks are now noise.

Pros.

  • Trivial to implement.
  • Predictable token budget per chunk.
  • Cheapest possible ingest.

Cons.

  • Splits mid-sentence and mid-word.
  • Breaks back-references. A chunk that says "the controller does X" no longer tells you which controller, because the noun it referred to is in a different chunk.
  • Splits tables, code, equations down the middle.
  • Embeddings get noisy because chunks are not self-contained.

When to use. As the baseline you measure improvement against. Acceptable if your corpus is short, uniform, and pre-cleaned (chat transcripts where each turn stands alone, log lines with fixed structure).

When NOT to use. Anywhere else. Treat it as a demo strategy. If a tutorial proposes this for a production RAG, the tutorial is wrong.

Strategy 2: Recursive Character Splitting

Workflow. Try the most semantic separator first (a blank line between paragraphs), fall back to weaker ones (single newline, space, character-level splits) if chunks remain too large. LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter default separator list contains exactly four entries: paragraph break, line break, space, and an empty-string fallback.

How it works. If a paragraph fits within your chunk size, keep it whole. If not, drop down to sentences. If a sentence is still too long, drop to words. The hierarchy of separators is your knob.

Example. A blog post with clean paragraph breaks chunks naturally on the blank lines and you get clean paragraph chunks. A dense PDF dump with no paragraph breaks falls all the way through to space-splitting and you get fragments again.

Pros.

  • Cheap, fast, deterministic.
  • Respects paragraph and sentence boundaries when the document has them.
  • The "no-thought" choice that mostly works as a starting point.

Cons.

  • Document-blind. Does not understand that ## Section Header matters more than a regular newline.
  • Will still split inside tables and code unless you customise separators.
  • Treats every newline as equal weight.

When to use. Your default starting point on a new corpus. Run it before reaching for anything fancier. Strong baseline against which everything else must justify itself.

When NOT to use. As a permanent answer for structured documents (Markdown, HTML, code, layout-rich PDFs). It cannot exploit structure it does not see.

Strategy 3: Sentence-Based Splitting

Workflow. Use a real linguistic tokenizer (NLTK Punkt, spaCy) to detect sentence boundaries, then group sentences until you hit chunk size.

Why it matters. Naive splitting on the period character breaks "Dr. Smith arrived at 3 p.m. The talk lasted 45 min." into garbage. A real sentence tokenizer keeps the abbreviations, decimals, and ellipses intact.

Example. A legal contract: "Pursuant to Section 4.2, the Party shall remit payment to Acme Inc. within 30 days." Naive split breaks this into four pieces. NLTK Punkt keeps it whole.

Pros.

  • Handles abbreviations, decimals, ellipses without breaking mid-sentence.
  • Multilingual via spaCy models.
  • Cleaner inputs to downstream pipelines (sentence-window, propositions).

Cons.

  • Slower than character splitting (the sentence model has overhead).
  • Still document-blind. Ignores headings, tables, lists.
  • No help on code, JSON, HTML.

When to use. Prose-heavy corpora where abbreviation handling matters: legal text, narrative content, support tickets, transcripts.

When NOT to use. Code, structured data, or anywhere the structure carries more meaning than the prose.

Strategy 4: Document Structure-Aware Splitting

Workflow. Parse the document's native structure (Markdown headings, HTML tags, PDF layout, code AST) and split on the boundaries the author already wrote.

How it works. A MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter walks #, ##, ### and treats each section as a chunk. A PDF parser like Unstructured.io detects layout (titles, lists, tables, captions) and chunks by element type. A code splitter parses the AST and keeps functions and classes together.

Example. A pricing comparison table in a product doc stays as one chunk instead of being cut after row three. A Python class with five methods stays whole instead of being split mid-method. A ## Authentication section in an API doc stays self-contained, with its heading available as metadata for filtering.

Pros.

  • Tables stay intact - massive win for technical reference content.
  • Code functions and classes stay coherent.
  • Headings can be indexed as metadata for filtering and reranking.
  • Preserves what the author meant to be a unit.

Cons.

  • Requires a parser per format. PDF parsing is hard, OCR is harder.
  • Layout-rich PDFs need heavyweight tools (Unstructured.io hi-res, LlamaParse, Docling) which are slow and sometimes expensive.
  • Bad input markdown (no consistent heading levels) means bad chunks.

When to use. Always, as the bottom layer of your pipeline. Stack other strategies on top, never replace this floor. The single highest-ROI thing you can do for technical documentation, product manuals, regulatory filings, and code-heavy corpora.

When NOT to use. When your input is unstructured prose with no markup signal (raw transcripts, chat logs, OCR'd notes). Use sentence-based or recursive instead.

Strategy 5: Hierarchical (Parent-Child / Small-to-Big)

Workflow. Build a tree: document, then sections, then paragraphs. Embed the small leaves (paragraphs). At retrieval time, if multiple leaves under the same parent match the query, return the parent instead of the individual leaves. LlamaIndex calls this auto-merging.

How it works. You decouple the retrieval surface (small chunks, high precision) from the generation surface (larger chunks, full context). The retriever finds tight matches; the merger expands them upward when the matches cluster.

Example. API documentation. The query "how do I authenticate" matches three paragraphs under the ## Authentication section. Instead of returning three disjoint paragraphs, the retriever returns the whole section. The LLM gets coherent context; the user gets a coherent answer.

Pros.

  • Solves the precision-vs-context tradeoff explicitly.
  • Tight queries get tight chunks; broad queries get whole sections.
  • Decouples retrieval surface from generation surface, which is the right architectural call.

Cons.

  • More moving parts: tree storage, merge thresholds.
  • Storage roughly doubles or triples (you index leaves and store parents).
  • The merge threshold is a hyperparameter that needs evaluation.

When to use. Long documents with hierarchy: technical manuals, RFCs, regulatory filings, books, multi-section product docs. Anywhere the author already wrote a hierarchy and the query mix spans both narrow and broad questions.

When NOT to use. Short flat documents (tweets, FAQ entries, support tickets). There is no hierarchy to exploit and the added complexity buys you nothing.

Strategy 6: Semantic Chunking (the paper tiger)

Origin. Popularised by Greg Kamradt in his "5 Levels of Text Splitting" tutorial. Reimplemented as SemanticChunker in LangChain Experimental and SemanticSplitterNodeParser in LlamaIndex.

Workflow. Embed each sentence (or a sliding window of sentences). Walk the document; when adjacent-sentence cosine distance jumps above a threshold, insert a chunk boundary. The intuition is appealing: chunks should match topic boundaries, not character counts.

How it actually behaves in production.

  • Sentence embeddings are noisy on technical text. Vocabulary repeats across topics. The embedder sees the same words and reports high similarity even when the meaning has shifted. The signal it is supposed to fire on barely exists.
  • The threshold is a knob nobody tunes. Percentile, standard deviation, interquartile range - each has a different default and most teams ship the default. The default is wrong for any specific corpus.
  • You pay embedding cost at ingest with no guaranteed gain. Every sentence gets encoded, and the boundaries it picks are not provably better than the cheap separators recursive splitting already uses.
  • Independent evaluation pushes back hard. Chroma's 2024 chunking benchmark (linked at the bottom) ran the threshold-based semantic chunker against a recursive baseline and the recursive baseline was competitive or ahead on the metrics they reported. The marketing copy is stronger than the evidence. Their own clustering variant - covered next - did beat the baseline; pure threshold-walking did not.

Pros (theoretical).

  • Variable-sized chunks aligned to topic transitions.
  • No reliance on author markup.

Cons (in practice).

  • Loses to a far cheaper recursive baseline in independent evaluation.
  • Adds embedding compute at ingest with no consistent gain.
  • Brittle to threshold choice and embedder choice.

When to use. Honestly: only when you have measured a win on your specific corpus against a recursive baseline. The intuition is reasonable; the empirics are not on its side by default.

When NOT to use. As a default. The cost is real and the win is not consistent. If a vendor pitches it without showing you their eval, ask for the eval.

Strategy 7: Cluster Semantic Chunking

Workflow. Variant of semantic chunking that frames the problem as clustering instead of threshold-walking. Embed sentences, cluster them globally to maximise within-cluster similarity, treat the clusters as chunks. Proposed by Chroma in their 2024 chunking evaluation as their own novel method.

Why this beats the threshold-walking approach. Local pairwise similarity is fragile - one bad neighbour pair anywhere and the boundary is wrong. Clustering looks at the global structure of the document and is more robust to the noisy similarity signal that breaks the threshold approach.

Example. A research paper with related-work, methods, results, and discussion sections. Pairwise similarity may flicker as terminology recurs across sections. Clustering looks at the document holistically and groups sentences by their dominant theme.

Pros.

  • The semantic variant most worth trying, per Chroma's own evaluation.
  • More stable than threshold-walking.
  • Still no reliance on author markup.

Cons.

  • Still pays the embedding cost at ingest.
  • Reference implementation is research code, not yet a stable library API.
  • Gains are corpus-dependent. You still have to evaluate.

When to use. If you are committed to a semantic approach and want the variant with the best independent evidence behind it.

When NOT to use. Before you have beaten the recursive baseline on your own eval set. Fix that first. Cluster semantic is a refinement, not a foundation.

Strategy 8: Late Chunking

Workflow. Push the entire document through a long-context embedder first. Then split. Mean-pool the token vectors per chunk to get the chunk embedding. The split happens after the encode, not before.

How it works. In naive chunking, each chunk is embedded in isolation, blind to the rest of the document. With late chunking, every token vector has already attended to the whole document via the transformer's self-attention, so the chunk vectors carry context that the chunks themselves do not contain in their text.

Example. A long Kubernetes doc. A chunk reading "the controller watches for changes and reconciles state" is ambiguous on its own across Deployments, StatefulSets, and custom operators. With late chunking, the token vectors for that sentence already encode "this section is about StatefulSets" because they attended to the surrounding section. The chunk gets retrieved for the right resource, not the wrong one.

Pros.

  • Resolves cross-chunk references at the embedding level rather than the text level.
  • Gains track document length: the longer the document, the bigger the win.
  • Composes with any chunk-boundary algorithm above. You still pick where to split, you just embed differently.
  • The text content of the chunk is unchanged, so storage stays the same.

Cons.
