entry.title: Artificial Intelligence & SAP EPPM
entry.content:
AI is fundamentally reshaping SAP Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management (EPPM) by enabling intelligent automation, predictive analytics, and data-driven decision-making across the full project lifecycle. By integrating capabilities such as machine learning, natural language processing, and generative AI, SAP EPPM is evolving from a traditional project tracking platform into a proactive, intelligent management system — helping organizations align project investments with strategic objectives, optimize resources, and mitigate risks before they escalate.

Key AI Capabilities in SAP EPPM

 

  • Intelligent Project Navigation (NLP): The Joule Copilot's Navigational Project Assistant uses natural language processing to allow users to find and open applications displaying project data, directly access projects, Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements, or networks in the Project Builder — reducing navigation effort and improving user satisfaction. 
  • AI-Driven Resource Skill Matching: The Advanced Skill Match feature in SAP Project and Resource Management (SPRM), powered by SAP Business AI, automates skill matching and similarity analysis — identifying candidates with exact or similar competencies and ranking them by relevance and availability. 
  • Predictive Risk & Early Warning Detection: Agentic AI can detect early warning signals across project variables, helping teams mitigate risk before issues escalate. 
  • Automated Portfolio Scoring & Prioritization: AI-powered solutions (e.g., built on SAP BTP integrated with large language models) automate the scoring, simulation, and prioritization of project portfolios, replacing subjective manual scoring with data-driven evaluation. 

How AI Enhances Core EPPM Processes

Portfolio Management

AI enables dynamic portfolio prioritization by aligning project plans with strategic objectives. Solutions built on SAP BTP can automate portfolio scoring and simulate project outcomes under different conditions, empowering finance teams and portfolio managers to make informed decisions about which initiatives to pursue, postpone, or cancel. This reduces governance bottlenecks and improves financial transparency.

Risk & Issue Management

A unified data foundation within SAP EPPM enables Agentic AI to dynamically prioritize portfolios and detect early warning signals across project variables — assisting teams to mitigate risk proactively before issues escalate. Additionally, machine learning-based scenarios (such as Risk of Late Payment) predict which customers are most likely to miss payment deadlines, with AI explainability features available to analyze scenario trends. 

Joule Copilot

Intelligent AI assistant embedded in SAP Fiori launchpad for project navigation, planning, and sales order monitoring

SAP Project and Resource Management (SPRM)

Cloud-based resource management tool with AI-driven skill matching for ETO and PSA projects

Business Benefits

  1. Enhanced Efficiency: Faster staffing processes through AI-driven skill matching reduce project delays and administrative overhead. 
  2. Improved Decision-Making: Automated portfolio scoring and live simulations enable more informed, data-driven investment decisions — reducing governance bottlenecks. 
  3. Proactive Risk Mitigation: Early warning signal detection across project variables allows teams to address risks before they escalate into issues. 
  4. Improved Resource Utilization: AI aligns workforce capabilities with project needs, ensuring the right people are assigned to the right roles. 
  5. Operational Cost Reduction: Organizations implementing AI-enhanced SAP solutions have experienced considerable reductions in operational costs within the first year of deployment, stemming from automation of labor-intensive processes and reduction of manual errors. 
  6. Better User Experience: Natural language navigation via Joule reduces effort for accessing project data and improves overall user satisfaction.

Future Outlook

  • Agentic AI is emerging as a key direction for SAP EPPM, with intelligent agents capable of dynamically prioritizing project portfolios, aligning plans with strategic objectives, and detecting early warning signals autonomously
  • SAP AI Agents focused on sales, supply chain optimization, pricing strategies, and inventory management are upcoming additions to SAP's AI ecosystem.

"AI is transforming SAP EPPM from a passive project tracking tool into an intelligent, proactive management platform that supports the full project and portfolio lifecycle"

entry.title: Factoring AI into Your Procurement Strategy
entry.content:
Driving Real Procurement Transformation: Beyond Software and AI 🌐

In a recent episode of SAP’s Future of Procurement podcast, Jay Brand, Director of Solution Advisory for Spend and Finance, shared candid insights into what truly drives successful procurement transformations. While technology—especially AI—dominates today’s headlines, the conversation underscored a critical truth: transformation is not delivered by software alone. It is achieved through clarity of purpose, strong leadership, disciplined execution, and ongoing commitment to improvement. 🚀

Software Isn’t Magic—Strategy Comes First 🧭

A central theme in the discussion was the misconception that software alone can deliver transformation. Organizations often invest heavily in technology expecting immediate results, only to be disappointed when outcomes fall short. As Brand highlighted, tools are only as effective as the strategy behind them.

True transformation starts with a clear understanding of business objectives, measurable KPIs, and the organizational will to change. Without this foundation, even the most advanced solutions become underutilized. Leaders must define the “why” before investing in the “how,” ensuring alignment across stakeholders and a shared vision for success. 📊

Avoiding the Feature Trap ⚙️

Another common pitfall is the tendency to get lost in feature-level comparisons during solution evaluation. Teams often focus on minor system differences—interface preferences or configuration details—rather than assessing how a platform supports broader business goals.

Brand emphasized that this “feature-first” mindset can derail transformation efforts. Instead, organizations should prioritize outcomes over perfection, avoiding analysis paralysis. Decision-making should be streamlined, guided by a small group of empowered stakeholders, and focused on achieving measurable value rather than replicating legacy processes. 🔍

Transformation Is a Continuous Journey 🌱

One of the most compelling analogies shared during the podcast likened transformation to caring for a plant. Organizations invest heavily during implementation—watering, nurturing, and monitoring progress. However, once systems go live, attention often fades, leading to stagnation or failure.

Successful companies recognize that transformation doesn’t end at go-live. Continuous improvement, regular optimization, and ongoing user engagement are critical. Cloud solutions, in particular, require iterative enhancements, frequent reviews, and adaptation to evolving business needs. Maintaining this momentum ensures long-term value realization. 🔄

AI: Powerful, but Not a Shortcut 🤖

The rise of artificial intelligence has introduced both opportunity and unrealistic expectations. While AI holds significant promise for procurement, Brand stressed that it is not a “magic bullet.” Organizations expecting AI to instantly solve complex challenges often overlook the foundational work required.

Effective AI adoption begins with clearly defined problems. Companies must articulate specific use cases rather than asking broad questions like “What can AI do for us?” Without this clarity, AI initiatives risk becoming unfocused and ineffective. 🎯

Data: The Foundation of AI Success 📈

A recurring insight was the critical role of data in unlocking AI’s potential. Clean, structured, and interconnected data is essential for meaningful insights and automation. Many organizations have accumulated large volumes of data but lack the strategy to manage or leverage it effectively.

Brand emphasized that data quality—not just quantity—determines AI success. Poor data leads to inaccurate outputs, which can have serious business consequences, especially in areas like forecasting or supply chain planning. Establishing a robust data strategy is therefore a prerequisite for any AI-driven transformation. 🧠

Integrated Ecosystems Over Fragmented Solutions 🔗

When considering procurement transformation, organizations face a choice between best-of-breed tools and integrated solution suites. The podcast highlighted a growing trend toward integrated ecosystems, particularly in the age of AI.

Unified platforms provide consistent data models and seamless process integration, enabling more advanced analytics and automation. This holistic approach enhances visibility, reduces complexity, and supports enterprise-wide optimization. While niche solutions may address specific needs, they often lack the broader context required for strategic decision-making. 🏗️

Balancing Innovation with Realism ⚖️

A key takeaway was the importance of setting realistic expectations. While innovation is essential, organizations must recognize that enterprise-grade solutions evolve at a measured pace. Providers are balancing cutting-edge capabilities with reliability, scalability, and business-critical accuracy.

Transparent communication between vendors and customers is vital. By setting clear expectations, demonstrating current capabilities, and outlining a roadmap for future innovation, organizations can build trust and ensure alignment throughout the transformation journey. 🤝

The Future of Procurement: Evolution, Not Revolution 🔮

Looking ahead, Brand offered a pragmatic perspective on the future of procurement. While technology will continue to evolve, the core principles of procurement—relationship management, strategic sourcing, and value creation—will remain constant.

AI and automation will augment, not replace, human expertise. Procurement professionals will continue to play a critical role in decision-making, supplier relationships, and strategic planning. The future lies in blending advanced technology with human insight to drive sustainable value. 👥

Final Thoughts 💡

Procurement transformation is not a one-time initiative but an ongoing journey requiring strategic clarity, disciplined execution, and continuous improvement. Technology and AI are powerful enablers, but their success depends on foundational elements—clear goals, strong data, and organizational commitment.

As organizations navigate this evolving landscape, the winners will be those who balance ambition with realism, leveraging innovation while staying grounded in core business principles. The question is not whether to transform—but how thoughtfully and effectively that transformation is pursued. ✅

Listen on APPLE!

Listen on Spotify!

entry.title: Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator
entry.content:
Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator

Introduction

In SAP integration projects, technical design documents are critical for capturing interface details, design decisions, mapping rules, adapter configurations, error handling, and operational monitoring. However, creating these documents manually for every SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CPI, interface can be time-consuming and repetitive.

This blog explains a practical use case for building an AI-powered application that can generate SAP CPI technical design documents automatically using inputs such as manual interface details, iFlow packages, and mapping sheets.

The goal is not to replace solution architects or developers, but to accelerate documentation creation and provide a consistent first draft that can be reviewed, edited, and approved by the project team.

Business Problem

SAP CPI developers often spend significant effort documenting integration flows after development is complete. A typical technical design document includes:

  • Interface overview
  • Source and target system details
  • Adapter configuration
  • Mapping and transformation logic
  • Error handling
  • Retry and reprocessing strategy
  • Monitoring and logging
  • Security and authentication
  • Testing scope
  • Deployment checklist

In large integration programs, the same documentation structure is repeated across many interfaces. This creates an opportunity to use AI to generate a structured draft based on available technical inputs.

Proposed Solution

The proposed solution is an AI-assisted SAP CPI TDD Generator.

The application allows users to enter basic interface details, upload an SAP CPI iFlow package, optionally attach a mapping sheet, and generate a technical design document in Word format.

The generated document can include:

  1. Technical design document content
  2. Architecture diagram
  3. Mapping sheet appendix
  4. Test scenarios
  5. Deployment checklist
  6. Assumptions and dependencies

The user can review and edit the generated output before downloading the final document.

High-Level Architecture

The application consists of the following components:

 

Key Features

  1. Manual TDD Generation

The user can provide basic information such as:

  • Interface name
  • Source system
  • Target system
  • Sender adapter
  • Receiver adapter
  • Mapping logic
  • Error handling
  • Integration flow description

The AI model uses this information to generate a structured technical design document.

  1. TDD Generation Using iFlow Package

The application can accept an exported SAP CPI iFlow package in ZIP format. The backend extracts relevant files such as:

  • .iflw
  • XML files
  • Groovy scripts
  • XSLT mappings
  • WSDL files
  • XSD files
  • Properties files

This extracted information is passed to the AI model to generate a more accurate document.

  1. Mapping Sheet Attachment

The user can upload a mapping sheet in Excel or CSV format. The application reads the mapping data and includes it in the generated Word document as an appendix.

This is useful for documenting source-to-target field-level transformations.

  1. Architecture Diagram Generation

The application generates a basic architecture diagram showing the integration flow:

Source System → Sender Adapter → SAP CPI → Mapping/Transformation → Receiver Adapter → Target System

It also includes monitoring, logging, and error handling as part of the diagram.

  1. Word Document Export

The final output is generated as a Word document. The document includes:

  • Interface details
  • Architecture diagram
  • AI-generated TDD content
  • Mapping sheet appendix

This makes the document easy to review, modify, and share with project stakeholders.

 

Technology Stack

A simple prototype can be built using the following technologies:

Component

Technology

Frontend

Streamlit

Backend

Python

AI Model Access

OpenRouter / OpenAI-compatible API

Document Generation

python-docx

Mapping Sheet Processing

pandas, openpyxl

Diagram Generation

Pillow

File Processing

zipfile

This stack is lightweight and suitable for a quick proof of concept.

Sample User Flow

  1. User opens the Streamlit application.
  2. User selects one of the generation options:
    • Manual Entry
    • Generate using iFlow Package
    • Generate using iFlow Package + Mapping Sheet
  3. User provides interface details.
  4. User uploads the iFlow ZIP file.
  5. User uploads the mapping sheet, if available.
  6. User clicks Generate TDD.
  7. The application extracts technical details.
  8. The AI model generates the TDD content.
  9. The user reviews or edits the generated document.
  10. The user downloads the final Word document.

Prompt Engineering Approach

The application uses a controlled prompt to reduce hallucination. The prompt instructs the AI model to behave as an SAP CPI Integration Architect and generate a technical design document using only the provided information.

Example instruction:

Use the iFlow package details where available.

Use the mapping sheet details where available.

Only use information provided by the user, iFlow package, or mapping sheet.

Do not invent missing information.

If any information is missing, mention "To be confirmed".

Do not include passwords, secrets, API keys, or credentials.

This approach helps ensure that the generated document remains reliable and does not include unsupported assumptions.

Generated TDD Sections

The generated technical design document can include the following sections:

  1. Document Purpose
  2. Interface Overview
  3. Business Requirement Summary
  4. Architecture Diagram Description
  5. Source System Details
  6. Target System Details
  7. SAP CPI iFlow Design
  8. Sender Adapter Configuration
  9. Receiver Adapter Configuration
  10. Message Mapping and Transformation Logic
  11. Routing Logic
  12. Exception Handling
  13. Retry and Reprocessing Strategy
  14. Security and Authentication
  15. Monitoring and Logging
  16. Externalized Parameters
  17. Mapping Sheet Summary
  18. Assumptions
  19. Dependencies
  20. Testing Scope
  21. Test Scenarios
  22. Deployment Checklist
  23. Appendix Reference

Benefits

This solution can help integration teams by:

  • Reducing manual documentation effort
  • Improving consistency across TDD documents
  • Accelerating handover from development to support
  • Creating a reusable documentation framework
  • Helping junior developers understand CPI interface design
  • Reducing missing sections in technical documentation
  • Enabling faster review cycles

Important Considerations

While AI can accelerate documentation, generated content should always be reviewed by a developer, solution architect, or technical lead.

The application should not expose or store sensitive information such as:

  • Passwords
  • API keys
  • Client secrets
  • Private certificates
  • Production credentials

For enterprise usage, additional controls should be added, such as:

  • Authentication and authorization
  • Audit logging
  • Secure file handling
  • Data masking
  • Approved AI model usage
  • Document versioning
  • Human approval workflow

Possible Enhancements

Future enhancements can include:

  • Direct connection to SAP Integration Suite APIs
  • Automatic extraction of adapter properties
  • Advanced iFlow XML parsing
  • Interface version comparison
  • Confluence integration
  • Jira integration
  • SAP BTP deployment
  • Approval workflow
  • Reusable client-specific TDD templates
  • Automated test case generation
  • Compliance checks against integration design standards

Conclusion

An AI-powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator can significantly improve productivity in SAP integration projects. By combining SAP CPI artifact extraction, mapping sheet analysis, AI-generated documentation, architecture diagram generation, and Word export, teams can create high-quality first drafts of technical design documents in minutes.

The key principle is to use AI as an accelerator, not as the final authority. The best approach is:

Extract facts → Generate draft → Review and edit → Approve and publish

This use case demonstrates how AI can be practically applied in SAP Integration Suite projects to reduce repetitive effort and improve documentation quality.

Source Code

GIT Repository : https://github.com/jacksnh/sap-cpi-ai-tdd-generator

entry.title: Beyond Basic AI Error Handling in SAP CPI - A Rules-First Architecture with HANA Cloud Learning
entry.content:
The Problem Every Integration Developer Knows

Every SAP CPI developer has lived this scenario.

An iFlow fails in production at an inconvenient hour. You open the MPL log, stare at a cryptic stack trace, and spend the next 30–45 minutes manually cross-referencing adapter configurations, checking credentials, and testing payloads. By the time you identify the root cause  a null field in a mapping, an expired OAuth token, a malformed XML payload  the fix takes five minutes. The diagnosis took an hour.

Recent SAP Community posts have explored using the AI Adapter to automate this diagnosis, and the concept is genuinely sound. But the most common approach  sending the raw exception directly to an LLM has five problems that make it unsuitable for real production environments:

ProblemReal Impact
Raw payload sent to LLMBusiness data leaves the tenant — GDPR risk
Every error hits the LLMAuth failures and timeouts: unnecessary API cost at scale
No deduplicationSame error 1000 times = 1000 API calls
Free-text responseCannot be routed, filtered, or acted on programmatically
Response goes back to senderDevelopers never see the diagnosis

This post describes a different architecture — one that classifies errors deterministically first, sanitizes all business data before any AI call, caches intelligence in SAP HANA Cloud, and uses AI only for errors that are genuinely novel.

In my testing: 3 of 4 error types never touched the LLM at all.

What I Built: The ErrorAnalyzer Agent

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent is a native SAP CPI package  two iFlows, 13 Groovy scripts, a HANA Cloud free-tier instance, and the SAP AI Adapter configured with a breakthrough setting that is completely undocumented in SAP's official documentation.

Package contents:

  • EA_TestHarness — the main analyser iFlow, triggered via ProcessDirect
  • EA_VictimFlow — an example source iFlow that deliberately triggers errors to demonstrate the full pipeline

Technologies used:

  • SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)  iFlows, Groovy scripts, JDBC adapter, AI Adapter, Mail adapter
  • SAP HANA Cloud  free tier, with NLP vector engine enabled for V2
  • OpenRouter  LLM gateway (free tier models available)
  • OpenAI  via OpenRouter for AI diagnosis

Architecture Overview

The diagram below shows the complete three-zone architecture. Zone 1 handles detection inside SAP CPI. Zone 2 contains the intelligence layer — classification, sanitization, and dedup logic. Zone 3 is the AI and persistence layer.

 

 

The key design principle: rules first, AI only when necessary. Known errors (auth failures, timeouts, malformed payloads) are handled deterministically in milliseconds with zero LLM cost. Only genuinely novel or complex errors reach Claude.

Prerequisites

Before building, ensure you have the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite trial or production tenant with Cloud Integration capability enabled
  • OpenRouter account  free tier available at openrouter.ai (no credit card required for free models)
  • SAP HANA Cloud free tier instance provisioned on BTP (covered in setup steps below)
  • Gmail or SMTP account for email notifications
  • Postman or any REST client for testing

Step 1 — Security Material Setup

Before configuring any adapter, create two security artifacts in CPI Monitor.

1a. OpenRouter API Key

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → Secure Parameter

Field Value

NameOpenRouter_API_Key
TypeSecure Parameter
ValueBearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxxxx    ← include "Bearer " prefix — this is critical

Click Deploy. Wait for status to show Deployed  before proceeding.

Important: Do not store only the API key. Store the complete value including the "Bearer " prefix with a space. The AI Adapter uses this as the entire Authorization header value. This is part of the undocumented configuration covered in detail in Step 4.

 

1b. HANA Cloud User Credentials

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → User Credentials

Field Value

NameHANA_CLOUD_DB_USER
TypeUser Credentials
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour HANA instance DBADMIN password

Step 2 — HANA Cloud Setup

2a. Provision the HANA Cloud Instance

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → SAP HANA Cloud → select hana-free plan. Open HANA Cloud Central → Create Instance → SAP HANA Database. Enable the Natural Language Processing (NLP) feature in Advanced Settings  this activates the vector engine for V2 semantic search.

Free tier note: The instance stops every evening automatically and is deleted after 30 days of inactivity. Restart it manually when needed from HANA Cloud Central.

2b. Configure JDBC Data Source in CPI

Navigate to: Monitor → Manage JDBC Material → JDBC Data Source tab → Add

Field Value

NameHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Database TypeSAP HANA Cloud
JDBC URLjdbc:sap://<your-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443?encrypt=true&validateCertificate=true
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour DBADMIN password
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

Click Deploy → Test Connection. You should see a green success confirmation before proceeding.

 

2c. Create the Database Tables

Open HANA Cloud Central → SQL Console → connect using DBADMIN credentials → run the following SQL:

CREATE TABLE ERROR_PATTERNS (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    EXCEPTION_CLASS  NVARCHAR(255),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    ENVIRONMENT      NVARCHAR(20),
    AI_SUMMARY       NVARCHAR(500),
    ROOT_CAUSE       NVARCHAR(1000),
    RESOLUTION_STEPS NCLOB,
    SEVERITY         NVARCHAR(10),
    AUTO_RECOVERABLE NVARCHAR(5),
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    LAST_SEEN        TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FIRST_SEEN       TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    MPL_ID           NVARCHAR(100),
    RESOLVED         NVARCHAR(5)     DEFAULT 'false'
);

CREATE TABLE ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    LAST_ANALYZED    TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    CACHED_ANALYSIS  NCLOB
);

Step 3 — Build the EA_TestHarness iFlow

Create a new Integration Flow in your package. The complete flow structure is shown below.

 

 

The flow has the following step sequence:

  1. ProcessDirect Sender — address: /erroranalyzer/triage
  2. Content Modifier (setParams) — sets iflowName, environment, llmModel as Exchange Properties
  3. Classifier — Groovy script, categorises the error
  4. Sanitizer — Groovy script, masks payload values and generates fingerprint
  5. Router 2 — routes Non-LLM vs LLM-eligible errors
  6. DedupCheck — Groovy script, builds SELECT SQL for HANA lookup
  7. JDBC_SELECT Request Reply — executes SELECT against ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  8. ProcessDedupResult — Groovy script, parses JDBC XML response
  9. Router 3 — routes cache hit vs cache miss
  10. BuildCachedResponse / BuildPrompt — builds response from cache or builds LLM request
  11. Request Reply → AI Adapter — calls Claude via OpenRouter
  12. ParseLLMResponse → BuildEnhancedResponse — structures the AI analysis
  13. BuildEmailBody → Mail Receiver — sends HTML alert email
  14. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  15. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

Content Modifier — setParams configuration

Add a Content Modifier as the first step after Start. In the Exchange Property tab, add:

Property Name Type Value

   
environmentConstantDEV
llmModelConstantopenai/gpt-oss-120b:free  ← externalized, change without redeployment

Step 4 — The Classifier Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Classifier. This is the core of the rules-first approach — pure pattern matching, zero API cost, millisecond response.

Category Trigger Action Hits LLM

CONNECTIVITYConnectException, timeout, connection refusedRETRY_BACKOFFNo
AUTH_FAILUREHTTP 401/403, unauthorized, forbiddenREFRESH_CREDENTIALSNo
PAYLOAD_MALFORMEDSAXParseException, malformed XMLDEAD_LETTERNo
MAPPING_FAILUREMappingException, cannot convert, XSLT errorANALYZEYes
UNKNOWNEverything elseANALYZEYes
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    String exClass = (json.exceptionClass ?: "").toString()
    String exMsg   = (json.exceptionMessage ?: "").toString()

    String category; String action; boolean needsLLM = false

    if (exClass.contains("ConnectException") ||
        exMsg =~ /(?i)(timed out|timeout|connection refused|no route to host)/) {
        category = "CONNECTIVITY"; action = "RETRY_BACKOFF"
    } else if (exMsg =~ /(?i)statusCode:\s*40[13]/ ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(unauthorized|forbidden|invalid credentials)/) {
        category = "AUTH_FAILURE"; action = "REFRESH_CREDENTIALS"
    } else if (exClass.contains("SAXParse") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(content is not allowed in prolog|malformed)/) {
        category = "PAYLOAD_MALFORMED"; action = "DEAD_LETTER"
    } else if (exClass.contains("Mapping") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(xslt|mapping|cannot convert)/) {
        category = "MAPPING_FAILURE"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    } else {
        category = "UNKNOWN"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    }

    message.setProperty("errorCategory",    category)
    message.setProperty("suggestedAction",  action)
    message.setProperty("needsLLM",         needsLLM.toString())
    message.setProperty("exceptionClass",   exClass)
    message.setProperty("exceptionMessage", exMsg)
    message.setProperty("rawPayload",       (json.payload ?: "").toString())
    if (json.mplId)       message.setProperty("mplId",       json.mplId.toString())
    if (json.iflowName)   message.setProperty("iflowName",   json.iflowName.toString())
    if (json.environment) message.setProperty("environment", json.environment.toString())
    return message
}

Step 5 — The Sanitizer Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Sanitizer. This script parses the raw payload — XML or JSON — and replaces every leaf value with a typed token before anything leaves the tenant.

A customer record like this:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "ORD-001",
    "amount": null,
    "customer": "John Smith",
    "taxId": "DE123456789"
  }
}

Becomes this — safe to send to any external LLM:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "[STRING:7]",
    "amount": "[EMPTY]",
    "customer": "[STRING:10]",
    "taxId": "[STRING:11]"
  }
}

The Sanitizer also generates a fingerprint: MD5(iFlowName + exceptionClass + errorCategory). Including iFlowName ensures that the same MappingException in two different iFlows produces different fingerprints — preventing wrong cross-iFlow cache hits.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlParser
import groovy.util.XmlNodePrinter
import groovy.util.Node
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import java.security.MessageDigest

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String raw = ((message.getProperty("rawPayload") ?: "").toString()).trim()
    String sanitized
    try {
        if (raw.startsWith("<")) {
            def root = new XmlParser().parseText(raw)
            maskXmlNode(root)
            def sw = new StringWriter()
            new XmlNodePrinter(new PrintWriter(sw)).print(root)
            sanitized = sw.toString().trim()
        } else if (raw.startsWith("{") || raw.startsWith("[")) {
            def obj = new JsonSlurper().parseText(raw)
            sanitized = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(maskJson(obj)))
        } else if (raw.isEmpty()) {
            sanitized = "[EMPTY]"
        } else {
            sanitized = maskValue(raw)
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        sanitized = "[UNPARSEABLE: ${e.message}]"
    }
    String fpSource = (message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "").toString()
    message.setProperty("sanitizedPayload", sanitized)
    message.setProperty("fingerprint", md5(fpSource))
    return message
}

void maskXmlNode(Node node) {
    if (node.children().any { it instanceof Node }) {
        node.children().each { if (it instanceof Node) maskXmlNode(it) }
    } else { node.setValue(maskValue(node.text())) }
}

def maskJson(obj) {
    if (obj instanceof Map) { def m = [:]; obj.each { k, v -> m[k] = maskJson(v) }; return m }
    else if (obj instanceof List) { return obj.collect { maskJson(it) } }
    else { return maskValue(obj == null ? "" : obj.toString()) }
}

String maskValue(String v) {
    if (v == null || v.trim().isEmpty()) return "[EMPTY]"
    v = v.trim()
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+/)                return "[INT:${v.length()}]"
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+\.\d+/)           return "[DECIMAL]"
    if (v ==~ /\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}.*/) return "[DATE]"
    if (v ==~ /(?i)(true|false)/)     return "[BOOL]"
    return "[STRING:${v.length()}]"
}

String md5(String s) {
    MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5").digest(s.bytes).encodeHex().toString()
}

Step 6 — Router Configuration

Add a Router step after the Sanitizer. Configure two routes:

Router 2 — routes based on whether LLM is needed:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (LLM path)${property.needsLLM} = 'true'Non-XML
Non_LLM (default)Default Route — checkedN/A

Router 3 — routes based on HANA cache result:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (cache hit)${property.cacheHit} = 'true'Non-XML
Default (cache miss)Default Route — checkedN/A

 

 

 

Step 7 — HANA Dedup Check via JDBC

On the LLM path, before calling Claude, the system checks HANA Cloud. Add these three steps in sequence:

  1. DedupCheck — Groovy script that builds the SELECT SQL
  2. Request Reply → JDBC Receiver — executes the query against HANA
  3. ProcessDedupResult — parses the XML response from JDBC

Important: CPI's JDBC adapter returns query results as XML — not JSON. The structure is ROOT → select_response → row. Use groovy.util.XmlSlurper (not groovy.xml.XmlSlurper — that class does not exist in CPI's Groovy runtime).

// ProcessDedupResult — parse JDBC XML response
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    try {
        def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(body)
        def rows = xml.select_response.row
        if (rows && rows.size() > 0) {
            def row = rows[0]
            String cachedAnalysis = row.CACHED_ANALYSIS.text()
            String occCount = row.OCCURRENCE_COUNT.text() ?: "1"
            if (cachedAnalysis) {
                message.setProperty("cacheHit", "true")
                message.setProperty("cachedAnalysis", cachedAnalysis)
                message.setProperty("cacheOccurrenceCount",
                    (occCount.toInteger() + 1).toString())
            } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
        } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    } catch(Exception e) { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    return message
}

JDBC Receiver Adapter configuration for both SELECT and WRITE steps:

Field Value

JDBC Data Source AliasHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

 

Step 8 — The Undocumented AI Adapter Configuration

This section is the most important technical finding in this post.

If you have tried connecting SAP's AI Adapter (Custom-OpenAI-REST) to OpenRouter or any external LLM endpoint, you have almost certainly encountered this:

401 Unauthorized — Missing Authentication Header

It does not matter how many times you redeploy the Secure Parameter. Basic mode simply does not work with OpenRouter. After extensive testing, I identified the exact combination of three settings that resolves this completely.

Fix 1 — Configuration Type: Advanced (not Basic)

In the Processing tab, change Configuration Type from Basic to Advanced. Basic mode uses SAP's internal OpenAI abstraction layer which is incompatible with OpenRouter's authentication format. Advanced mode gives direct REST control.

 

 

 

Fix 2 — Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter

Store the full Authorization value — not just the key — in the Secure Parameter: Bearer sk-or-v1-xxx. The adapter uses this as the complete Authorization header value. Without the prefix, the header is malformed and the request fails.

Fix 3 — Split the URL across tabs

Connection tab and Processing tab must share the URL:

  • Connection tab Address: https://openrouter.ai
  • Processing tab Relative URL: /api/v1/chat/completions

 

Complete Working Configuration

Connection Tab:

Field Value

Message ProtocolCustom-OpenAI-REST
Addresshttps://openrouter.ai
API Key AliasOpenRouter_API_Key
Connection Timeout60000 ms
Response Timeout60000 ms

 

Processing Tab:

Field Value

Configuration TypeAdvanced ← Critical — Basic fails with 401
HTTP MethodPOST
Relative URL/api/v1/chat/completions
Request Payload SourceExchange Body
Request HeadersHTTP-Referer|X-Title|Content-Type

 

Step 9 — Build the LLM Request (BuildPrompt)

The BuildPrompt Groovy script assembles the request body for Claude. It reads the sanitized context from message properties — never from the raw payload — and forces a structured JSON response format.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String errorCategory = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "UNKNOWN"
    String exClass       = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String exMsg         = message.getProperty("exceptionMessage") ?: ""
    String sanitized     = message.getProperty("sanitizedPayload") ?: "[EMPTY]"
    String iflowName     = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown"
    String environment   = message.getProperty("environment") ?: "Unknown"
    String llmModel      = message.getProperty("llmModel") ?: "anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku"

    String userContent = """\
Analyze this SAP CPI runtime error and respond with ONLY valid JSON.

Required structure:
{
  "error_summary": "one clear sentence",
  "root_cause": "specific technical cause",
  "resolution_steps": ["step 1", "step 2", "step 3"],
  "auto_recoverable": false,
  "severity": "HIGH"
}

Severity: HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW only.
auto_recoverable: boolean only.

Error details:
iFlow: ${iflowName}
Environment: ${environment}
Category: ${errorCategory}
Exception class: ${exClass}
Exception message: ${exMsg}
Sanitized payload structure (real values masked):
${sanitized}"""

    def requestBody = [
        model: llmModel,
        messages: [
            [role: "system", content: "You are an SAP CPI integration expert. Respond only with valid JSON."],
            [role: "user",   content: userContent]
        ],
        temperature: 0.2,
        max_tokens: 400
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(requestBody))
    return message
}

Step 10 — EA_VictimFlow: Wiring the Exception Subprocess

EA_VictimFlow demonstrates how to connect any existing iFlow to the ErrorAnalyzer. The pattern is reusable — copy the exception subprocess to any failing iFlow by changing only the iflowName constant.

 

Add a Content Modifier at the start of EA_VictimFlow with these Exchange Properties:

Property Name Type Value

iflowNameConstantEA_VictimFlow
environmentConstantDEV

Inside the Exception Subprocess, add a Groovy script named ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def exCaught   = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")
    String exClass = exCaught ? exCaught.getClass().getName() : "Unknown"
    String exMsg   = exCaught ? exCaught.getMessage() : "Unknown error"
    String mplId   = message.getHeader("SAP_MessageProcessingLogID", String.class) ?: "unavailable"
    String iflowName = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown_iFlow"
    String payload = ""
    try { payload = message.getBody(String) ?: "" } catch(Exception e) { payload = "" }

    def envelope = [
        exceptionClass:   exClass,
        exceptionMessage: exMsg,
        payload:          payload,
        mplId:            mplId,
        iflowName:        iflowName,
        environment:      message.getProperty("environment") ?: "DEV"
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(envelope))
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")
    return message
}

Connect the ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder to a ProcessDirect Receiver with address: /erroranalyzer/triage

Important: SAP_ApplicationID is NOT the iFlow name — it is the application binding ID. Always use a Content Modifier constant for iflowName as shown above.

Step 10b — Writing to HANA Cloud

After Claude returns the analysis, two separate JDBC write steps persist the intelligence to HANA. They are intentionally split into two steps because CPI's JDBC adapter executes exactly one SQL statement per call — chaining two statements with a semicolon throws a syntax error.

PrepareHANAWrite — writes full analysis to ERROR_PATTERNS

This script builds a MERGE statement that either inserts a new error record or increments the occurrence count if the fingerprint already exists. Note the HANA-specific syntax: WITH PRIMARY KEY is required — standard WHERE syntax fails with a syntax error.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llm = new JsonSlurper().parseText(
                  message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}")

    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")    ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory")  ?: ""
    String exClass     = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")      ?: ""
    String environment = message.getProperty("environment")    ?: ""
    String mplId       = message.getProperty("mplId")          ?: ""
    String aiSummary   = (llm.error_summary   ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String rootCause   = (llm.root_cause       ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String steps       = JsonOutput.toJson(llm.resolution_steps ?: []).replace("'","''")
    String severity    = (llm.severity         ?: "MEDIUM").toString()
    String autoRec     = (llm.auto_recoverable ?: false).toString()
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_PATTERNS
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, EXCEPTION_CLASS, IFLOW_NAME,
         ENVIRONMENT, AI_SUMMARY, ROOT_CAUSE, RESOLUTION_STEPS,
         SEVERITY, AUTO_RECOVERABLE, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
         LAST_SEEN, FIRST_SEEN, MPL_ID, RESOLVED)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${exClass}',
                '${iflowName}','${environment}','${aiSummary}',
                '${rootCause}','${steps}','${severity}',
                '${autoRec}', 1,'${now}','${now}','${mplId}','false')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

PrepareDedup — writes fast lookup entry to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llmRaw         = message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}"
    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")   ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")     ?: ""
    String cached      = llmRaw.replace("'","''")
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, IFLOW_NAME,
         LAST_ANALYZED, OCCURRENCE_COUNT, CACHED_ANALYSIS)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${iflowName}',
                '${now}', 1,'${cached}')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

The complete write sequence after Claude analysis:

  1. BuildEnhancedResponse — assembles the final JSON response
  2. BuildEmailBody — builds the HTML alert email
  3. Mail Receiver — dispatches the email notification
  4. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  5. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  6. End

Both JDBC_WRITE and JDBC_WRITE2 use the same receiver adapter configuration as JDBC_SELECT  Data Source Alias: HANA_Cloud_JDBC, timeouts: 30 seconds.

Email is dispatched before the HANA writes so that if HANA is unavailable (free tier stops nightly), the developer notification still goes out regardless.

Step 11 — Testing

Deploy both iFlows. Test using any REST client by POSTing to the EA_VictimFlow HTTPS endpoint with plain text body:

Request Body Error Category Expected Behaviour

mappingMAPPING_FAILUREHits Claude, writes to HANA, sends email with AI analysis
authAUTH_FAILURENo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
timeoutCONNECTIVITYNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
xmlPAYLOAD_MALFORMEDNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email

Results

First Run — MAPPING_FAILURE (new error, hits Claude)

{
  "fingerprint":     "dfb60537dd72852be164ee7f46bab45a",
  "mplId":           "AGoi2tHxrNFx-9CloVUzH46UcB-6",
  "iflowName":       "EA_VictimFlow",
  "environment":     "DEV",
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "suggestedAction": "ANALYZE",
  "sanitizedPayload": "{\"order\":{\"id\":\"[STRING:7]\",\"amount\":\"[EMPTY]\"}}",
  "servedFromCache": "false",
  "llmParseSuccess": "true",
  "aiAnalysis": {
    "summary":   "Mapping failed because null Amount field cannot be converted to decimal",
    "rootCause": "Source system sent null where decimal value was expected",
    "resolutionSteps": [
      "Validate Amount field in source payload before mapping",
      "Add null check in Groovy mapping script before decimal conversion",
      "Define default value for optional numeric fields"
    ],
    "autoRecoverable": false,
    "severity": "HIGH"
  }
}

 

Second Run — Same Error, HANA Cache Hit

{
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "servedFromCache": "true",
  "occurrenceCount": "2",
  "aiAnalysis": { ...same analysis returned instantly, zero LLM call... }
}

 

The real MPL ID is captured in every response — developers can jump directly to the processing log without searching.

HANA Cloud as a Learning Store

Every new error diagnosis is permanently stored. After running for several weeks:

-- Top recurring failures
SELECT IFLOW_NAME, ERROR_CATEGORY, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
       AI_SUMMARY, SEVERITY, FIRST_SEEN, LAST_SEEN
FROM ERROR_PATTERNS
ORDER BY OCCURRENCE_COUNT DESC;

-- All unresolved HIGH severity errors
SELECT * FROM ERROR_PATTERNS
WHERE SEVERITY = 'HIGH' AND RESOLVED = 'false'
ORDER BY LAST_SEEN DESC;

 

This gives you a persistent error intelligence dashboard that no standard CPI monitoring tool provides natively. The more errors the system sees, the richer this store becomes.

Email Notifications

Every error  known or novel  triggers an email alert to the development team. Known errors (auth, timeout, malformed) receive a lightweight notification with category and suggested action. Novel errors with Claude analysis receive the full HTML email with root cause, resolution steps, and MPL ID link.

 

 

Honest Limitations

This is a V1 implementation on a trial tenant. To be transparent:

  • Synchronous design: The LLM call adds 3–8 seconds to the exception path. Production deployment should decouple via JMS queue — not available on trial tenants.
  • Exception message not sanitized: The payload is fully masked but exception message text is sent as-is. Real SAP exceptions can embed field values. Exception message sanitization is planned for V2.
  • No auto-remediation: suggestedAction is informational only. Phase B adds deterministic auto-recovery actions (retry, credential refresh) for known error types.
  • Free model availability: OpenRouter free models go offline without notice. For production, use SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub or a paid OpenRouter tier.

What Is Next: Joule Studio Integration via MCP

Phase 2 exposes the ErrorAnalyzer as an MCP tool and connects it to SAP Joule Studio via the MCP Gateway announced at SAP Sapphire 2026. The goal: ask Joule in natural language — "What failed in my OrderProcessing flow in the last hour?" — and have it call the ErrorAnalyzer tool, query the HANA learning store, and return a conversational diagnosis grounded in your tenant's actual error history.

Phase 3 replaces the exact-match fingerprint with semantic vector similarity using HANA Cloud's NLP engine , already enabled on the instance. Similar errors that differ only in variable values will match correctly, dramatically improving cache hit rates and diagnosis quality.

Summary

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent demonstrates that AI error handling in SAP CPI does not have to be expensive, risky, or shallow.

  • A rules-first classifier keeps 75% of errors away from the LLM entirely — zero cost, millisecond response
  • A payload sanitizer ensures no business data leaves the tenant — the LLM sees shapes, not values
  • A HANA dedup cache means the same error is diagnosed once — every subsequent occurrence is instant and free
  • A HANA learning store accumulates error intelligence permanently — the system gets smarter and cheaper the longer it runs
  • The AI Adapter breakthrough — Advanced mode, Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter, split URL — is the practical finding I hope saves other developers the hours of debugging it cost to discover

If you build on this or encounter any issues replicating the AI Adapter configuration, please comment below. The complete Groovy scripts are available on request.

entry.title: AI-Powered Customer Experience in the Travel and Transportation Industry
entry.content:
Delivering Seamless, Personalized Journeys

When we talk about Travel and Transportation there is only one thing a business should focus on, and that is ‘end-to-end’ customer journey. Excellence in experience is what you strive for from the very first point of interaction to the destination. Travelers nowadays have very strict requirements, from booking a ticket to arriving at a destination, they expect fast, convenient, and prompt assistance when they face issues along the way. No matter how perfectly we design a system to meet the needs, there will always be situations where we can’t avoid. Delays, confusing booking systems, long customer service wait times and many more creates frustration for travelers.

AI (Artificial Intelligence) is reshaping how Travel and Transportation industry is doing business. It delivers more avenues to provide customer care aside from the typical communication channels such as email, short messaging services, and social media. AI provides smarter, faster and more personalized customer experiences leading to happier customers which entails to growth in revenue for the business. AI is no longer optional, it is now becoming a necessity – The question is, HOW?

Beyond Tickets and Timetables: How AI Orchestrates the Customer Journey

Previously, travelers preferred travel agents over booking apps, relied on printed tickets, and valued personal service and human interaction. However, mobile apps for bookings, check-ins, and payments are now widely used. Travelers also expect real-time updates and personalized recommendations that provide seamless, end-to-end experiences.

SAP delivers an ecosystem that provides all the tools needed to meet these expectations. Using Service Cloud Version 2, organizations can manage cases, complaints, and information requests efficiently. AI can respond within seconds, unlike traditional customer service processes that rely on manual handling of support tickets. AI-powered chatbots can also manage a high volume of customer conversations simultaneously.

Here is an example integration strategy,

Data Type

Target Systems

Description

Customer Profile

Sales, Service, Commerce and Marketing

Implement for centralize account management, B2B/B2C Partnerships and real time account updates

Booking

Commerce, Service, and Marketing

Leverage AI driven product recommendations to automatically suggest hotel accommodations, flight and transportation combinations

Browsing Behavior

Commerce, Sales, and Marketing

Create dynamic customer segments based on travel preferences, booking patterns and engagement history

Loyalty Status

SAP Loyalty Management Program, Commerce

A centralized loyalty platform enables businesses to manage diverse loyalty programs, rewards, tiers, and promotions across multiple brands and regions from a single system while maintaining local flexibility and control

Support Interactions

Service

Provides unified customer support across chat, email and social media

AI

Sales, Service, and Commerce

Leverage AI to provide contextual travel recommendations based on customer preferences

Commerce Cloud Intelligent Selling Services

Intelligent Selling Services (ISS) for SAP Commerce Cloud is an AI-powered enhancement that leverages machine learning and artificial intelligence to deliver personalized customer experiences and optimize booking strategies. This help in analyzing customer booking pattern and provide contextual data across customer touchpoint and can provide recommendations which can lead increase in revenue.

Commerce Cloud Travel Accelerator

This industry-specific solution is designed to help travel companies deliver omnichannel digital traveler engagement through SAP Commerce Cloud. Organizations that already have an existing SAP Commerce Cloud solution can leverage this add-on to tailor the platform to their unique business requirements. By utilizing this solution, companies can access real-time information to deliver personalized customer experiences, strengthen customer loyalty, and drive greater business value.

Loyalty Management Program through Integration

Of course, a customer will need a system to reward customers for coming back. it’s like earning points, perks, or special treatment when you repeatedly book with the same travel company, for that an integration to loyalty management program either our very own solution or a 3rd party solution can be used.

The Way Forward

With the Advanced Success Plan, we can help you achieve your business goals. As a starting point, we will create a service engagement plan that provides a tailored approach to meeting your KPIs. During this phase, we will also deliver sessions to help you set up Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, and Commerce Cloud while ensuring that Travel and Transportation industry best practices are followed.

With AI capabilities available across every solution, you can now categorize your customer base based on travel behaviors and patterns, as well as perform sentiment analysis on customer reviews and support tickets. The SAP Advanced Success Plan can serve as a strategic partner in helping you achieve your AI objectives. Our experts, backed by deep industry knowledge, can provide guidance on the most effective path forward.

To deliver services that are aligned with each customer's specific goals, we have organized our offerings into four phases: implementation, pre-go-live, post-go-live, and continuous improvement.

Implementation:

During the implementation phase, our focus is on providing adoption guidance to help you set up your solutions, from front-end applications to back-end systems. We work alongside your team to establish the core capabilities needed for a successful implementation and to ensure the solution is aligned with your business requirements.

This includes, but is not limited to, application user management, key user extensibility, the CX AI Toolkit, integrations, security considerations, and other essential platform capabilities. Our goal is to help you build a solid foundation that supports scalability, maintainability, and future growth while enabling your teams to get the most value from the platform.

Pre-Go Live:

In the pre-go-live phase, our focus is to validate and safeguard the solutions that have been built throughout the implementation. The goal is to make sure your systems are configured correctly, performing as expected, and ready when you go live.

This includes conducting detailed reviews of business configuration settings, evaluating system performance, validating integrations, reviewing security and user access setups, and assessing analytics and reporting capabilities. We also help identify potential risks, gaps, or areas for optimization before launch, ensuring that any issues are addressed proactively rather than after go-live.

In addition, we work with your teams to confirm readiness across key functional and technical areas, helping ensure that testing has been completed successfully, critical business scenarios have been validated, and the solution is aligned with your operational requirements. By performing an Adoption Checkpoint during this phase, we help reduce risk, improve system stability, and support a smoother go-live experience.

Post-Go Live:

During the post-go-live phase, we work closely with your team to ensure that recommendations and best practices identified throughout the implementation have been properly configured and are delivering the intended results.

As your users begin working in the production environment, new questions, opportunities for optimization, and minor challenges often emerge. During this stage, we provide continued guidance and support to help address those items, whether they are related to business processes, system configuration, integrations, extensibility, analytics, or overall solution adoption.

Our functional and technical experts remain available to review issues, provide recommendations, and help your team navigate any areas that require additional attention. We also help identify opportunities for further improvements and knowledge transfer, ensuring your organization is well-positioned to maintain, enhance, and scale the solution over time.

Continuous Improvement:

Finally, as part of the continuous improvement phase, we help ensure that you remain informed about new innovations and enhancements introduced through SAP release cycles. By staying up to date with the latest capabilities, you can continue to maximize the value of your solutions and drive ongoing business success.
